Generally it is not recommended that you do anything potentially short-term deleterious to your credit during the process of seeking a mortgage loan - such as opening a new account, closing old accounts, running up balances, or otherwise applying for any kind of loan (people often get carried away and apply for loans to cover furniture and appliances for the new home they haven't bought yet). You are usually OK to do things that have at least a short-term positive effect, like paying down debt. But refinancing - which would require applying for a non-home loan - is exactly the sort of hard-pull that can drop your credit rating. It is not generally advised. The exception to this is would be if you have an especially unusual situation with an existing loan (like your car), that is causing a deal-breaking situation with your home loan. This would for example be having a car payment so high that it violates maximum Debt-to-Income ratios (DTI). If your monthly debt payments are more than 43% of your monthly income, for instance, you will generally be unable to obtain a "qualified mortgage", and over 28-36% will disqualify you from some lenders and low-cost mortgage options. The reason this is unusual is that you would have to have a bizarrely terrible existing loan, which could somehow be refinanced without increasing your debt while simultaneously providing a monthly savings so dramatic that it would shift your DTI from "unacceptable" to "acceptable". It's possible, but most simple consumer loan refis just don't give that kind of savings. In most cases you should just "sit tight" and avoid any new loans or refinances while you seek a home purchase. If you want to be sure, you'll need to figure out your DTI ratio (which I recommend anyway) and see where you would be before and after a car refinance. If this would produce a big swing, maybe talk with some mortgage loan professionals who are familiar with lending criteria and ask for their opinion as to whether the change would be worth it. 9 times out of 10, you should wait until after your loan is closed and the home is yours before you try to refinance your car. However I would only warn you that if you think your house + car payment is too much for you to comfortably afford, I'd strongly recommend you seriously reconsider your budget, current car ownership, and house purchasing plans. You might find that after the house purchase the car refi isn't available either, or fine print means it wouldn't provide the savings you thought it would. Don't buy now hoping an uncertain cost-saving measure will work out later.

Well you 've asked a question that has many , many answers . You 've been accepted ! Have you already chosen your future career ? If so , the process changes greatly based on what you want to be . You may end up as a full time student for just 2 years , or 4 , or 6 , or 8 , or 12 . And that is if you do not change your mind , or fail , or drop any classes . You may go to school for 4 years , then get a job and go to school part time another 2 - 3 . You may have to take a series of standardized tests to move on , or you may have " on - the - job " education requirements . Each profession in the US has a different path . I 'd say most common is the 4 year Bachelors degree . You can simply earn a general education bachelors degree and forge your own path . Or you may want to become an engineer - that is a challenging 4 year degree many people extend to 5 years . Or maybe you were thinking the medical profession - you have tiers of nursing : LPA ( 1 yr ) , RN ( 2 - 4yr ) , Nurse Practicioner(4 - 6yr ) , Doctorate of Nursing ( 6 - 12yr ) , or you may want to be a doctor ( 10 - 14 yr depending on specialty ) . All of these different professions have slightly different paths to employment too : engineering and business degrees typically include internships ; low paying jobs you take during your final year or after graduation . The point of these is to impress the employer so they hire you full time . Other degree fields may rely more on entrepreneurship or simply finding a job right out of the gate . All in all , if you have a degree in mind most major Universities will provide a detailed path for you , and there will be a subreddit here under the degree name .

I think the best ELI5 would be to equate a computer as a teacher , and computer programs as little students . So ... a little kid asks for a marker from the teacher . Teacher hands it out . When the student is finished with the marker , they return it to the teacher - and the teacher puts it back into the marker jar . Now what ends up happening , is that there 's * a lot * of little kids asking for markers . At once . Teacher dutifully hands them out . Now , little kids being little kids , some * forget * that they already have a marker and ask for another one . They inadvertently keep the first marker , while using the second marker . Sometimes they forget they have two , and then ask for a third . So when the teacher runs out of markers , she freaks the fuck out . Yells at all of the kids , kicks them out of the room , scours the whole room for markers , and puts them back . Now imagine each " marker " being a piece of memory . A memory leak occurs when the little shits ( programs ) forget that they have markers yet keep asking for more , and the teacher knows that they exist somewhere but not sure * exactly * where . You reclaim memory ( markers ) when you refresh everything and start over .

Assuming that no one else has hit the ask, and the asks are still there, yes, you will fill $54.55 as long as you didn't exhaust that ask. Actually, there is no "current price at which the stock is exchanging hands"; in reality, it is "the last price traded". The somebody who negotiated prices between buyers & sellers is the exchange through their handling of bids & asks.  The real negotiation comes between bids & asks, and if they meet or cross, a trade occurs. It's not that both bid & ask should be $54.55, it's that they were. To answer the title, the reasons why the bid and ask (even their midpoint) move away from the last price are largely unknown, but at least for the market makers, if their sell inventory is going away (people are buying heavily and they're running out of inventory) they will start to hike up their asks a lot and their bids a little because market makers try to stay market neutral, having no opinion on whether an asset will rise or fall, so with stocks, that means having a balanced inventory of longs & shorts.  They want to (sometimes have to depending on the exchange) accommodate the buying pressure, but they don't want to lose money, so they simply raise the ask and then raise the bid as people hit their asks since their average cost basis has risen. In fact (yahoo finance is great about showing this), there's rarely 1 bid and 1 ask.  Take a look at BAC's limit book: http://finance.yahoo.com/q/ecn?s=BAC+Order+Book You can see that there are many bids and many asks.  If one ask is exhausted, the next in line is now the highest.  The market maker who just sold at X will certainly step over the highest bid to bid at X*0.9 to get an 11% return on investment.

I like to always start with my credentials , or reason that I can answer this question . I have been in business for along time and am part of a global business leadership program plus studied business at the Univ . of Connecticut . I also worked as a manager in an outsourced contract center in Mexico . In cost accounting we attempt to break down the cost of a product . Take any product , say a lollipop . The are material costs ( the candy , the sticks , the wrapper ) . There are labor costs ( the guys who run the machines that make this , the guys who pack it etc ) . Then there is GSA ( general selling and administrative expenses ) . A business leader seeks to maximize gross profit margin , that is the amount of money applied to the operating income after the costs of goods is subtracted . When the business leader analyses the costs that go into making the lollipop they may find that the labor costs are contributing a disproportionately high percentage . The decision may be made at that point to lower labor costs by sending the labor to an outsourcing center , where the labor cost per unit will be considerably lower . The idea is to increase the gross profit margin . It does not always work but in a labor intensive operation it usually can if managed properly . It is really just a matter of mathematics when it comes to whether or not it is a good deal to do . Now for my personal opinion . In most cases I do not feel that outsourcing hurts America . In many ways the argument can be made that it helps America . The majority of jobs that are sent overseas are non - skilled . This does two things . It keeps consumer prices down , which is good for the economy overall and it forces the American people to seek new and better ways to earn a living . I do not want the next generation to depend on a vocation that can easily be done by unskilled laborers overseas . I would rather they learn a skilled labor vocation . Now people usually automatically react to outsourcing as stealing American jobs . I can say that at one time I was a partner business owner that manufactured a product where the contribution margin was razor thin . We wanted to keep the business in Houston , TX . It was absolutely necessary to keep labor costs low . We posted job positions for line workers and material handlers at the labor cost that would sustain the business and received no responses form " typical Americans " . Our only choice was to either move the manufacturing south of the border or not be in business at all . We did not go into business to fail , but to make profit for our stakeholders . We ended up hiring first generation immigrants who were willing to work for the lower wage . Remember that in the labor market workers only have one of two things to offer : a special skill or the willingness to do something that anyone can do for less money .

There were a few conditions that set up the French Revolution . Firstly , food prices were high , and there was a lot of starvation and malnutrition . The harvests in the years prior to the Revolution were quite poor . This was not 100 % the Monarchy 's fault , but it did destabilize the people . Secondly , France was nearing bankruptcy . The government lost a lot of money during the Seven Years War and the American Revolution . Thirdly , the ideas of freedom and democracy were getting more popular . The Enlightenment was taking place around this time , and a lot of intellectual Frenchmen were inspired by the American Revolution . These three factors combined is what caused the dominoes to topple . The first actual event that led to the French Revolution was the calling of the Estates - General in 1789 . Because of the financial crisis , the King summoned the Estates - General to help him find a solution . This was the first time since 1614 that the Estates - General were summoned , a sign to many that the Monarchy was falling apart . The Estates - General was divided into three parts : the clergy , the nobility , and the peasant / commoners . When they voted , each Estate had one vote . This was unfair however , since about 97 % of the population ( the Third Estate ) had only 33 % of the vote . If the clergy and the nobility teamed up , which they mostly did , the majority of France would be left in the cold . So after the earlier meetings , the Third Estate decided to split away and form their own assembly . This act basically led the rest of the peaceful and also violent acts of the Revolution . There are many effects of the French Revolution . Firstly , they were the first country in mainland Europe to overthrow the Monarchy . It was used as a blueprint for many future revolutions , such as the rebellions of 1848 and the Russian Revolution . Nationalism was born during the Revolution . People were fighting not for a king or for a god , but for their country . Nationalism would heavily influence politics until the end of WWII . The Revolution also plunged the whole of Europe into war . It really depends on which historian you ask , but it can be argued that Napoleon was a product of the Revolution , and kept going until 1815 , 26 years after the Revolution started . Because of Napoleon , many countries were created and destroyed . The end of Napoleon was also the start of the Concert of Europe . To prevent future problems , the Monarchs of Europe decided to protect each other from further uprisings . This led to almost 40 years of peace in Europe . The French Revolution is also one of the first time a government had separated itself from religion . During the Revolution , land was removed from the Church and France rejected Rome 's influence . They even changed the names of the months ( until Napoleon changed it back ) because they referenced pagan gods . EDIT : Spelling and grammar

I just explained this is another post , but 's here 's a copy . The idea of representative democracy is that you vote 1 person per group of people to speak for you on national issues . It 's easier for one person to represent 10000 people 's opinions ( a republic ) than for everyone to vote on every single issue ( a democracy . ) In our case , it 's about 650 people representing 300+M Americans . But , history has been shown that elected representatives are not perfect bastions of infinite integrity . Like normal people , they have weaknesses and can be swayed and influenced by things other than altruism and persuasive argument . Some learned that if you give money to those representatives , you can get them to vote for things they may not have voted for otherwise . Thus , people with a lot of money may be able to influence the laws of the country and get laws passed that the people of the country may not really wanted or needed . So to prevent this conflict of interest , we passed laws that say you can not directly give money to a politician ( I.E. I ca n't give my congressman $ 1 M and say " I 'll pay you $ 1 M to vote no " ) . Instead , you can give money to their " campaign fund " , meaning the money they use to advertise themselves for reelection . Laws also state how that money can be used ( they ca n't use it to pay for their home or kids tuition , for example . ) The laws limited how much money a person or company could give to a candidate , so people created a " Political Action Committee " , a group that people could give more money to that was directed for similar purposes as a campaign fund . Laws also require that all donations in excess of a certain amount must be listed publicly so people can see who is funding a politician 's campaign . PAC 's can be used for single candidates , or for a group of candidates ( I.E. one candidate in a primary , or the R or D candidate in a national race . They can also represent multiple candidates , and the multiple candidates are allowed to give recommendations on how they 'd like the money spent . ) But , because some people / organizations wanted to put MORE money into campaigning , they created a SUPER - PAC . These campaigns are NOT authorized OFFICIALLY by the candidate or political party , but rather are organizations for trying to persuade the public on certain issues . Because these do not give their money to specific candidates or parties , and because the candidates nor their campaigns are not in control of these SUPER PACs , the laws that govern how much you give to these organizations is NOT limited . This is only possible because of the recent Supreme Court cases ( Citizen 's United ) . So now , you can still donate money to a Politician 's campaign if you want them to get elected . You can donate to their political action committee , but there are limitations on the amount . You can , however , donate unlimited amounts of money to a SUPER - PAC , and SUPER - PACs are not required to list who their donors are . Remember the Swiftboat ads ? They were required to say " Paid for by Swift Vote Veterans of America " at the end . Swiftboat was also required to list the donors to their ads to anyone that requests them . SuperPAC ads do n't have to list donors . They only have to list the name of the SuperPAC . The problem that people see is that they think that Super - Pac 's , while not OFFICIALLY under the control of a candidate , will actually work very closely with a candidate and their campaign , which essential side - steps the whole purpose of why we made laws to limit the amount of money people could donate in the first place . This debate falls under the subject of " campaign finance reform " . some people see Super - PAC 's as a bureaucratic veil that makes it easy to follow the letter of the law while entirely ignoring the spirit of the law . SO , what does this have to do with Stephen Colbert ? He is trying to show people how something like campaign finance reform - a subject which may seem boring and abstract to every day people - is really an important issue . He can now use the money he has collected to create signs , billboards , commercials for or against any political candidate without their permission - and he does n't have to say who paid for it . It is likely that Stephen may use the money to make actually commercials that make certain candidates look really foolish , or sarcastically make them look amazing - all of which will be apparent to the viewer . If enough people start to care about campaign finance reform , then it is more likely that new laws will be created to remedy some of the perceived problems . * * TL;DR * * Here 's a short metaphor : Steve wants to get elected , so Steve 's friends donate the max amount to his campaign . However , Steve still does n't have enough money to win against his opponent , Dave . So one of Steve 's friends , Chris , creates a SUPER - pac " * * F**riends * * o**f * * A**merica " . Now , Steve 's friends can donate more money toward running ads against Dave to help Steve get elected by donating money to " Friends of America " . Steve does n't " control " how FoA 's money gets spent- Chris does . But Chris and Steve are close buddies , and people suspect that Steve is still essentially telling Chris how to spend all the money in the SUPER - pac , even though the money is n't technically Steve 's . People suspect that Steve is actually controlling Friends of America and sidestepping the law , but because FoA does n't have to divulge who its donors are , there 's no way to see whether Steve is using the organization inappropriately . So Steve could be using FoA as an extension of his regular compaign funds , but there 's no way to prove it . Hence , one argument about SUPER - Pacs - that they are a legal technicality that completely bypasses all the rules about how money can be given to candidates , because they do nt have to disclose who paid for them - with money , gifts , or otherwise .

In general, prices are inversely proportional to rates; however, accurate interest rate prediction would make one worthy of managing a large credit derivative hedge fund. This is not to say that interest rates cannot go up in Canada since the world is currently undergoing a resource bust, and the United States has begun exporting more oil, even trying to recently open the market to Europe, both of which Canada is relatively dependent upon. Also, to say that Canada currently has the most overpriced real estate is an oversight to say the least considering China currently has entire cities that are empty because prices are too high. A ten to twenty percent drop in real estate prices would probably be a full blown financial crisis, and since mortgage rates are currently around 2.5%, a one to two hundred basis point rise could mean a nearly 50% decrease in real estate prices if interest payments are held constant.  Canada would either have to start growing its economy at a much higher rate to encourage the central bank to raise rates to such a height, or oil would have to completely collapse suddenly to cause a speculatively possible collapse of CAD to encourage the same. The easiest relationship to manipulate between prices and rates is the perpetuity: where p is the price, i is the interest payment, and r is the interest rate.  In this case, an increase of r from 2.5% to 4.5% would cause a 44.5% decrease in p if i is held constant.  However, typical Canadian mortgages seem to mature in ten years at a fixed rate, so i cannot be held constant, and the relationship between r and p is less strong at earlier maturities, thus the most likely way for prices to collapse is for a financial collapse as described above.

You 're at camp , and the camp leaders are lazy . They only want to pay attention to where one specific camper is . But they still need to be able to find any of the campers if asked , so they need the campers to each keep track of some of the other campers . Linked List : each camper keeps track of one other camper . So if they need to find a certain camper they ask the one camper they pay attention to to tell them where the guy he keeps track of is . They go down the chain until they find the guy they 're looking for . A " doubly linked list " means you keep track of the guy who keeps track of you and a new guy , and the new guy keeps track of you and another guy , so that they can go forward or backward along the chain . Binary Trees : The main camper they pay attention to knows where two campers are , and they know where he is . They each know where two more are , and so on until everyone is kept track of . The camp leaders want to find someone and so they ask their guy if he knows . He looks at his two guys to check if they 're the right ones , and if so , reports back to say the answer . Otherwise he asks them to find the guy . Just like he did , each of them check their two guys and if one of them is the right one , they tell the guy keeping track of them where he is , and then he tells the leaders . Otherwise they each ask their two guys , and so on . Eventually you have to either find the right guy or get down to the guys who are n't keeping track of anyone new . So he must be one of them , and it gets reported all the way up to the first guy , who tells the leaders .

Huge prime numbers are useful for cryptography as multiplying them together very easy calculation to do one way and really hard to do the other . For example , 13 times 9 equals 117 . That is simple to do . Let 's say you make a password system that does an equation to unlock the login , and the answer has to match 117 . in your system you store a person 's password as 13 rather than the actual password , and then times that by 9 . If it 's correct , you will have 13 times 9 which equals 117 . It is much safer than matching password123 to see if it directly matches password123 . If someone gets into your system , they can just see everyone 's password , while if you used prime number equation they would only see the number 117 . Let 's take it from the hackers point of view with a different number . What two prime numbers , multiplied together , equals 7781 ? The only one you can eliminate straight off the bat is that it ca n't be number 2 , as it 's an odd number , . Well , what happens when I divide it by three ? No , I get 2593.66666 ... , so that is n't correct , maybe if I tried seven ... The only way to find out what two prime numbers equal 7781 is to go through and divide it by every single prime number till you get a result that is also a prime number . It will take a bit of time to figure out it 's 31 times 251 . Longer the prime number you are dealing with , the more computational time it will take for any hackers to figure out what prime numbers are being used .

* * A 2D TRIANGLE * * Start with flat 2D geometry : Put 2 points , A and B , on a piece of paper , and draw a straight line between them , and measure length of line from A to B. Next , add a 3rd point , C , anywhere on the paper , and connect that to points A and B to make a triangle . Can you make the line from A to C to B as short as the line from A to B ? The only way to do that is to put C on the line between A and B. At that point , though , it ceases to be a triangle , and is really just the line from A to B with another mark on it . * * DEFINING " GREAT CIRCLE " * * Now , let 's try the same experiment on a 3D sphere . When working in 2D , we only needed 2 starting points . With 3D , we need 3 starting points . If point A is Sydney , point B is Johannesburg ( A and B could be any other two different points on the sphere , as well ) , and point C is the center of the sphere ( Hey ! C for center ! That works out well ! ) , you can imagine 1 flat surface going through each of those points , dividing the sphere into 2 parts . Further , because this flat surface goes through the center of the sphere , you also know that the flat surface is dividing the Earth exactly in half . In other words , if you took the sphere apart where its cut by that flat surface , the edge would not only be circular , but it would be the largest possible circle you could make with that sphere . It 's the greatest possible circle size , or " great circle " for short . The shortest distance between any two points on that circle , is along that great circle itself . * * SHORTEST POSSIBLE DISTANCE PROOF * * Want proof ? OK , we 're going to do that triangle experiment again , but this time on the surface of the sphere ! Points A ( Sydney ) and B ( Johannesburg ) are already on the great circle , of course , because that 's how we started . Choose a point D that 's on the surface of the sphere , but not on the great circle . Points A , B , and D now form a spherical triangle ( a triangle on the surface of a sphere ) . Just like in the flat triangle version , the only way to get the line from A to D to B to be as short as the line from A to B is to move D so that it 's on the line from A to B. There 's your proof that the shortest distance along the surface of a sphere between any two points on that surface is along the great circle ! * * BONUS TRIVIA * * Planes flying from San Francisco to Tokyo fly a great circle path , but planes flying from Tokyo to San Francisco do not . Planes flying from Tokyo to San Francisco can save fuel by taking advantage of the jet stream over the Pacific .

Well , stocks are just 1 part of " The Market " . It would take me days to explain to you different aspects of how the market works ; people get PhD 's in Finance and still ca n't fully comprehend certain aspects of how it moves . For the sake of conversation , let 's stick with stocks ( equity ) . You can go to yahoo finance and type in a 4 letter ticker symbol to find the stock price of any publicly traded company . The price of that stock is reflective of how the market feels about that company 's future . This price is based on earnings , expectations , current state of that industry , news etc . Even stupid things like the CEO getting caught cheating on his wife can cause a fluctuation in the value of a company 's stock . Just remember , something only has value if someone is willing to pay for it . The value of a stock is the value a buyer is willing to pay for it , and the value a owner is willing to sell it for . The fluctuation of the stock $ is a combination of attitudes of buyers & sellers . First thing I like to tell people about the stock market is the theory of Market Efficiency explained by Miller & Modigliani * which , in a nutshet , says that a stock 's current price reflects ALL information , both insider and public knowledge . If you think you can beat the market , you 're wrong , because once you know about the information , so does the rest of the world , and stock prices adjust immediately .

Nerves work ( partially ) by transmitting voltages . A voltage applied directly to nerves can stimulate them , even if the current is low . So pain nerves fire , and nerves that control muscles can fire . ( I imagine that other nerve receptors can fire too , but our brains focus on pain . ) Electrostatic discharge ( ESD ) can have very high voltage ( thousands of volts ) and a surprising amount of current ( Amps ! ) , but only for a brief time . Even if an ESD event goes across your heart , it is not going to have enough total energy to do it for very long ( times are typically measured in nanoseconds or microseconds ) . This is because ESD sources typically do n't have a lot of charge to make current available . But as we all know , you can get a shock from ESD that is painful enough to startle you . But to do actual damage ( or put your heart into fibrillation ) there has to be both voltage and current ( power ) , for enough time to inflict damage . If the voltage is too low , there wo n't be much current due to the contact resistance and the resistance of the body ( Ohm 's Law , V = IR ) . A high voltage almost guarantees a high current ( unless you are poorly grounded or the contact resistance is high ) , but it does n't guarantee that it will travel through the heart . If currents are very high and there 's enough total energy , tissues can be damaged by the current . As current travels through a conductor ( you ) that has resistance , its energy gets dissipated in the form of heat . The heat that is released is proportional to I^2 * R ( or I*V ) .

Let me take a crack at this from a slightly more generalized perspective . * * To understand the Vietnam War is to understand the Cold War * * . In 1939 when World War II broke out , there were a handful of major powers in the world that were primarily located in Europe : UK , France , Germany . Russia which had just become the Soviet Union was inherently powerful because of their massive size and population . The USA was an emerging power and Japanese Empire was on a rampage of conquest in Asia . While the USA and the USSR were allies during WWI ( as Russia ) and WWII , the USSR 's participation on the Allies ' side was not so much because of strong relationships with the other western powers but because of betrayal from Germany . Also , the USSR essentially fought the eastern European front by themselves which led to an inherit segregation between the western allies of the USA and western European powers and the USSR . ( btw , there are way more historical reason we can dive into even prior to WWII that separates Russia from the rest of the European powers but I think the point is made ) By the time WWII ended , the European powers were in shambles and Japan was neutered . This left just the USA and USSR as kind of a " last man standing . " Oh , and one other thing ; the USA developed the atomic bomb . This changed everything . Not wanting to fall to a clear second on the world stage to the USA , the USSR scrambled to develop atomic weapons of their own . The USA now had a new mandate which can be summarized into this course of logic : 1 . USA is good 2 . USA is a Democracy . 3 . So democracy is good ! which then meant ... 1 . Communism is the opposite of Democracy 2 . Communism is the opposite of good 3 . Communism is BAD ! And the opposite happened in the USSR . Not long after WWII ended , both the USA and USSR had atomic ( and eventually nuclear ) weapons . As such . they became the two remaining superpowers on the planet . So what happens next ? They split the world into the two sides : pro - USA or pro - USSR nations ( aka democratic / capitalist vs communist ) . There was absolutely no way that the USA and USSR could go to war with each other directly . When only one side had atomic weapons you ca n't mess with 'em because if they ever start losing , you 're getting nuked . Thus , a war between the USA and USSR would eventually lead to the use of atomic / nuclear weapons . So the logic became , " we 'll just have more bombs then them ! " Yeah , that did n't work out because all it did was made sure that if the war ever did happen both sides would just get the absolute hell nuked out of 'em and essentially end modern civilization . So how was the USA or the USSR supposed to beat the other side ? Remember the world was split between blue ( American democracy ) and red ( Soviet communism ) . You can fight the other side directly , but if the whole world gets painted blue or red : YOU WIN ! The USA and USSR focused their efforts on converting more and more countries to their side . This was done through political pressure , espionage , military action , etc . NATO and the Warsaw Pact was formed . The battlefield was drawn and the front lines were where democratic and communist countries shared borders . Both sides felt that to lose just one country could cause a domino effect in the region and instead of losing just one small country , like Korea or Afghanistan , they could possibly lose you the entire continent .. or worse ! This led to proxy wars throughout the 20th century where the USA and USSR would be going against each other , but never directly ( because it would lead to nukes and the end of everybody ) . This is why whenever there was a threat that a country would fall from one side to the other or there was an opportunity to bring one more country into your fold ( like in Korea , Portugal , Afghanistan , Nicaragua , Cuba , India , etc . ) , you can bet your ass one side was getting money and weapons from the USA and the other side from the USSR . * * This leads up to 1955 and the outbreak of the Vietnam War . * * The USA had been doing reasonably well in Asia , especially with strong allies in South Korea and Japan . Especially considering how much of Asia that the USSR and China take up . When communist forces started a revolution in Vietnam the USA saw it as another proxy war and had a duty to stop communism from taking Vietnam . * * This was a HUGE mistake on the USA 's part . * * While Americans saw it as another Democracy / Communism fight the Vietnamese saw it as a war of independence . Up until the Japanese conquest in WWII , it was a French colony for almost 100 years . When they saw the USA come in , they were n't seen as liberators but yet another country trying to impose their colonialism on them . Other posts have pointed out why fighting in the Vietnam was so difficult in terms of battle like environment and familiarity so I wo n't go into that . One of the most significant characteristics of the Vietnam War was that it was the very first where mass communications came into play and the American government did n't know how to handle it . It was the first war where public option played such a huge role . The American people were seeing raw photographs and video of the war and they were repulsed ( as ALL war is repulsive ) . To rebut some other posts here , as an American , yeah , we know we lost that one . Ironically the we made many of the same mistakes the British did during the American Revolution . I 'm not a cop , but a friend of my just finished his training and started about a month ago . He had this exact same question when he started but has since learned the answer . Should also preface this story with the fact that it obviously only completely applies to where we live , there are likely differences depending on where you live . So he went through his training and they had very stringent fitness requirements . During training they also give medals to officers who reach a certain level of fitness . If you receive that medal you are immediately put on a faster promotion track ( not considerably faster but still worth while ) . I think the idea being to try and get as many officers to pursue fitness as possible . According to him the problem once you are on the force is that it is never followed up . /u / donkeyholic and /u / Sheltopusik were on the right track . Officers spend a lot of time at desks doing paper work , in patrol cars etc ... Policing while requiring occasional sudden bursts of action is actually a very sedentary job . That combined with shift work makes it difficult for them to stay in shape . Apparently in our area there has been several attempts over the past 10 or 15 years to create physical requirements for cops , already on the job . The idea being to deal out reprimands or punishments to officers who ca n't meet them . Unfortunately as /u / Sheltopusik put it , unions . Under union regulations officers ca n't be punished or reprimanded for physical requirements . As long as they are still able to show up every day and , as my friend put it " fit in the cars or at their desks " their ok as far as the union is concerned . The problem if you boil it down is that they work sedentary jobs ( for the most part ) , work shift work and have no incentive to stay fit . The other problem you face is how would you deal out reprimands . You ca n't suspend officers without pay if one day you decide they all need to meet certain requirements if they did n't need to meet them the day before . A cop who is 5"11 and 275lbs is n't going to drop 100 pounds in a couple weeks , it 's gon na take a while . So if we really want to get cops in shape then forces will need to invest a lot of money into programs to help these guys along and new union rules will need to be passed . Anyway , long message but I thought I could weigh in , I had this discussion with my buddy only two days ago .

The only issue I have ever had with the Union is when I worked for a Memory ( RAM ) company based out of San Jose in the mid 90 's . When negotiations started for the new contract the union demanded Pay raises across the board based on the fact that the company had increased its profits every year for the last three years . The CEO combated that with " If we lose money for the next three years are you going to demand a pay cut across the board ? " Of course there was dead silence . In the end they agreed to a pay increase that was about 10 % more than then the cost of living increase . About 2 years later the internet bubble burst and sales dropped by 60 % in a three month time span . I got the hell out and went into the healthcare industry . When the time came to renegotiate the Union again asked for a cost of living increase , the CFO opened the books and showed them it was impossible , they had the numbers in their hands and still demanded it . The company was closed , everyone out of work . A few years ago I ran into the old CEO at a trade show , he drunkenly confessed that it was a more profitable move to close the company and sale the customer list and walk away . He was originally the one that brought the union in . I never personally had to deal with the union directly , but I did have to deal with poor employee 's that continued to work there because the union protected them . I am not saying this behavior is normal or on par with all unions , but it was the experience I had and it has left a bad taste in my mouth 10 + years later . I work in Health care now as an engineer , I work with several union employees that are rock stars at what they do , but I still have a bit of prejudiced against them because they are union . Maybe I will never get over it . Maybe I am in a different place , but it has been my experience that the more money I help the company make , the more money and free time they are willing to give me , I do n't need to threaten to strike if I do n't get a raise , Ill just leave if they do n't give me one . I would sooner die than allow someone to negotiate my pay for me , and I would personally be humiliated if my boss wanted me out but could not fire me because some lawyer was protecting me . Again maybe I am just fortunate in my life and do n't understand the plights of some . I wo nt say I am against the Labor Unions , but I do have some trust issues with them . Sorry OP the best I can do is offer you my experiences , I really ca nt answer as to why there is so much negativity towards the unions in the states .

Short answer : no . Longer answer : We used to have an ELI5 Guide to the Galaxy before the wiki system was ever integrated , which was basically a self post by a mod that was rarely updated with a few links to some good explanations for common topics . Here is why that does n't work anymore . There are a * ton * of topics , and sometimes having reposts can be a good thing . It makes room for fresh perspective , both timewise and just for people who have n't had the chance to speak up before . New explanations or a brilliant analogy can get a chance . In addition , who are we mods to determine which explanation for a topic like string theory or Keynesian economics is the " best , " and therefore gets to go on the wiki ? And who would be able to edit it ? These are just a handful of the concerns . Now here 's why it 's a good thing we do n't do this . * * This entire subreddit is an archive of answers . * * We have a search bar . Use it ! We do everything we can to have answered posts marked as such . This does n't hinder conversation , but it lets a casual browser now or in the future see at a glance what has already been explained-- much like a wiki . And yes , you too can edit it by writing up your own explanation or asking for clarification about a question that you feel was never properly answered in the past . There are sites like Simple Wikipedia that serve a similar purpose . But to be honest , having an ELI5 wiki would ultimately hinder actual content while going largely unread and causing controversy . * * * Also , please be sure to include " Meta " in your post the next time you ask about the subreddit itself !

Your assertion is false . Does America have the largest economy in the world ? Yes . Would we have that same # 1 economy if social welfare programs from the War on Poverty never existed ? This one is impossible to answer , but I think the answer is clearly no . Imagine after WWII if there had been no social security , no GI Bill , no Interstate highway system , but instead we went with only market based solutions to our problems . Some would be quick to respond that some of these have nothing to do with poverty and I would be quick to point out they have nothing to do with anything the government should be doing by looking at the Constitution . They are ways to get federal money out to improve things that no one else is improving . As others are pointing out , we saw much higher growth early on when democrats were the bigger Santa of the two parties and it declined as conservatives took power in the 80s to the point where growth is almost non existent today . That is not a simple story of one party good , other bad but I think it does point out that the methods to distribute federal dollars used by the GOP , tax cuts and increased defense spending , are not as effective at creating growth . And do n't believe that we have a small government party . That big military spending is in some ways to me a New Reconstruction that is supported by the more Dixiecratic wing of the the GOP and in many ways is not about security but about creating jobs which to me means lots of that GOP backed spending is part of a war on poverty even though most die hard " conservatives " would call me a socialist nutjob . I tend to retort that the modern GOP is about as fiscally conservative as a tuna sandwich . In conclusion , the War on Poverty has helped tens of millions of Americans lead better lives . The Welfare state had problems that needed fixing , but I think the GOP solution of transferring those dollars to defense sector jobs programs has made the Warfare state the new Welfare state and again we are at a point where we need to evaluate spending and eliminate waste because it is having only limited reach by tying it to defense .

I am in commercial real estate ... The issue with buying a real estate property ( unlike buying a baseball or common consumer good ) is that there could be issues with the property that you previously did n't know about . Common issues are : Asbestos , issues with the roof , mold , the property is on a fault line not previously known about , etc . When you purchase a property , you submit an LOI ( letter of intent ) . This says that you are saying you INTENDING to buy the property contingent upon X , Y , & Z happening . These contingencies can be anything from ' this house will pass all inspections ' to the ' property sale is contingent upon whether or not you get a desired loan ' . Oftentimes , the seller does n't sell a home to the highest bidder ; Rather , to avoid headache , the seller may sell the home to a buyer who will pay less but has less ( or shorter timed ) contingencies . Now getting to the escrow part : In between the time a seller accepts the bid and the time it takes for the bid actually going through , you are in what is called escrow . This is a time when all inspections must be done , when the loan must be set in place , etc ( basically X , Y & Z happening ) . If a term is not met , or the buyer does not complete the tasks he or she promised in the LOI , the building comes out of escrow and can be put back on the market if desired . If all of the tasks are completed , money is exchanged through escrow . In this , the buying party puts money into a separate bank account in escrow . When the tasks are completed , the money gets put in the seller 's bank account and the buyer recieves the title and all the goodies of the home . I like to think of escrow as purgatory , as it is right in the middle but not one way or the other . I hope this helps !

I ... I ca n't believe I know this . It 's fluid dynamics . As the urine travels down your urethra it is under almost equal pressure from all sides . As the liquid escapes it loses contact with the vessel . Through a process called adhesion the water in your product will experience a drag effect as the contact with the waste channel is extinguished . This drag is greatest in areas that contain the most perturbation . The shape of the orifice that produces the stream produces the vast majority of the perturbative influence . In this case the opening is a slit which produces semi - toroidal flows in the medium at the polar vertices . Combine these forces ( The sudden loss of pressure , the semi - toroidal flows , and adhesion ) and the net effect is a torsion force on the stream . The torsion force impels the fluid to twist and cohesion ( water molecules are kind of like tiny magnets and really want to stick together ) keeps the stream together . There you have it . Why your urine spirals instead of just flowing out like a garden hose . EDIT : changed adhesion to cohesion in the last bit , my mistake(I typed this at work and did n't take the time to proof - read ) . Also ; to those pointing out the navicular fossa you are correct in the biological sense . I was going for a my physics based approach , I apologize for not being more clear . Lastly ; thanks for the gold you wonderful redditors , you guys rock !

Once upon a time there was a river , and next to the river was a village . The river did not flow from north to south nor east to west . Neither did it flow from south to north nor west to east . It just flowed the way it did and the villagers never gave it a moment 's thought . This because all roads in the village lay either parallel to the river or at right angles to it . If a stranger appeared , say , asking directions to the wenchkeep , a villager might respond thusly : " you walk toward the river three blocks , along the river for four blocks , then away from the river for a block . " Then the villager would beam , proudly hooking thumb though a buttonhole in his woolen over - shirt , " there may you procure the finest wenches in all the land . " One day the King came to town . The King brought with him a Sorcerer and the Sorcerer brought with him a device . The device looked to be a pocket watch , but on the face , where the numbered hours should have appeared , were instead inscribed only the names of the four conjectural winds , in clockwise ordered North , East , South , and West . Above these floated a black needle , which by unknown majik did always point to North , irregardless of which however the Sorcerer aimed the thing . Clearly , this was an evil thing , and the villagers began to wonder aloud just what the heck NASA was doing with all those chickens and other tax sundry they had demanded over the years . The King raised a fisted glove , silencing the twittering of the crowd . " My people , " he began . " Henceforth shall ye navigate by this device , for it imposes upon the land a dividing up into north - south and east - west directions . You shall use these directions , and these directions are the ones shall you use . No more of this river direction talk , or whatever the heck it is you backwater yahoos call your primitive system of circumnavigation . " Upon hearing this a great roar came from the crowd . Ramsock the Barrel Maker roared loudest of all . " King , " he roared . " Your system is unnatural and will impose complexities that will kill our jerbs . Presently , should need arise to send barrels to the bagelry , I simply tell my barrel herders to go downstream for a furlong , then away from the river for seven blocks . With your new devilry , downstream will become south - east , away from the river will become south - west and toward the river north - east . Or is it south - south - east or north - south - west ? I know not , for who can reckon such things ? Who here today are capable of these Pythagorean machinations ? " Ramsock motioned to Dipthong the Miller . " You , Dipthong ? " And then to Klaxxon the Barkeep . " You , Klaxxon ? Verily , King , we can barely keep the more dolty amoungst us from coupling with the livestock ! " There followed a chorus of disparagement directed against the King , some of which was spittle - flecked and accompanied by obscene gesturing and demands both unnatural and anatomically improbable . You are attacking our values , Ramsock bellowed over the din , now beating a pitchfork against his breast . You are attacking eigen values ! he shouted , this time using his native tongue , which we 've conveniently assumed is Germanic for illustrative purposes . The King , though dismayed with this rather ham - fisted third act , recognized the need to wrap things up . Thus did he relent and permit the villagers to navigate according to their simple system of eigenvalues for the rest of their days . EDIT : [ source ] ( URL_0 )

So here is how our federal court system is structured ( stay with me for a minute ! ): at the lowest level we have trial courts called District Courts . They are usually the first courts to hear a certain case . Let 's say that I am a gay person and I sue the clerk of the court in my city for refusing me a marriage license . My case , Me v. Clerk , starts out in the District Court . We have a trial , the judge makes a ruling . The judge rules in my favor and decide 's my state 's ban on gay marriage is unconstitutional ! Yay ! If everything stopped here , gay marriage would happen in my jurisdiction , and the clerk would have to issue gay marriage licenses . But we are n't done . The Clerk can appeal the case to the next level , called the Circuit Court . This court gets to pass their own ruling on the issue . The Circuit Court rules in my favor ! Yay ! Again , if everything stops here , gay marriage can happen in my jurisdiction . Now , Clerk only has one option left . He has to appeal the case to the Supreme Court if he wants a different result . Here 's where Clerk runs into a problem . The main way to get a case into the Supreme Court is to file a petition for certiorari . This is a fancy way of saying " Hey Supreme Court , take a look at my case , it 's interesting and the states are all divided on what the law is , so can you settle this once and for all ? " The Supreme Court gets thousands of these petitions every year . They pick which ones to hear . Usually around 90 - 150 get heard . What happened here : The hypothetical Clerk petitioned the Supreme Court to hear the case to overturn the favorable gay marriage ruling from the Circuit Court . But the Supreme Court declined to take the case , which makes the Circuit Court 's ruling the last word on the case . Effectively , the matter ends at the Circuit Court 's ruling . And the Circuit Court struck down the gay marriage bans .

Figure out how much money you earn, what you spend it on, and how that will change when you have kids (will one of you stay at home? if not, how much will daycare cost and how do you finance the first few month when your child is still too young for daycare?) You will usually plan to spend your current Kaltmiete (rent without utilities) on your mortgage (the Darlehen that is secured by your house) - keep in mind though that a house usually has a higher utility cost than an appartment. When you've figured out what you can save/pay towards a house now and how that will change when you have kids, you can go on to the next step. If you don't want to buy now but want to commit to saving up for a house and also want to secure today's really low interest rates, consider getting a "Bausparvertrag".  I didn't find a good translation for Bausparvertrag, so here is a short example of how it works: You take a building saving sum (Bausparsumme) of 150000€ with a savings goal (Sparziel) of 50000€ (the savings goal is usually between 20% and 50% of the sum) and then you make monthly payments into the Bausparvertrag until you reach the savings goal at which point you can take out your savings and a loan of 100000 € (or whatever your difference between the Bausparsumme and Sparziel is). If you're living in an expenisve area, you're likely to need more than 150000 but this is just an example. Upsides: Downsides: If you decide to buy sooner, you can also use your Bausparvertrag to refinance later. If you have a decent income and a permanent job, then ask your bank if they would consider financing your house now. To get a sense of what you'll be able to afford, google "wie viel Haus kann ich mir leisten" and use a few of the many online calculators. Remember that these websites want to sell you on the idea of buying a house instead of paying rent, so they'll usually overestimate the raise in rents - repeat the calculation with rent raise set to 0% to get a feeling for how much you'll be able to afford in today's money. Also, don't forget that you're planning to get children, so do the calculation with only one income, not two, and add the cost of raising the kids to your calculation. Once you've decided on a property, shop around a bit at different banks to get the best financing. If you decide to buy now (or soon), start looking at houses now - go to model homes (Musterhäuser) to find out what style of house you like - this is useful whether you want to buy an existing house or build a new one. If buying an existing house is an option for you, start visiting houses that are on sale in your area in order to practice what to ask and what to look for. You should have a couple of visits under your belt before you really start looking for the one you want to buy. Once you're getting closer to buying or making a contract with a construction company, consider getting an expert "Bausachverständiger". When buying an existing house they can help you estimate the price and also estimate the renovation cost you'll have to factor in for a certain house (new heating, better insulation, ...). When building a new house they can advise you on the contract with the construction company and also examine the construction company's work at each major step (Zwischenabnahme). Source: Own experience.

This has already been answered fairly well , but just to fill out the historical context : Back in the day , floppy drives were your primary storage media , and hard drives were expensive and uncommon peripherals . You would keep your operating system on a floppy disk , and boot from that disk when you turned on your computer . Then you would switch disks to your program and load that afterwards . Yes , you literally installed your OS every time you turned the computer off . Did I mentioned hard drives were expensive ? There were so expensive that people would put up with this shit rather than buy them . Fancy computers would have * two * disk drives . That way you could boot from one floppy , and have a program disk in the other drive , without having to switch disks . Software assumed that the first drive was the floppy drive that contained the OS , which was labeled A. Secondary floppy drives were more common than hard drives , so if there was a B drive it was obviously a floppy drive . If you had a hard drive , you did n't want to confuse programs into thinking it was a disk drive so you labeled it C to avoid ambiguity . It was not until later that hard drives were considered normal . Many of the programs on those hard drives were written in the preceding years and were still generating business value , so the drive lettering was kept around to appease their quirks with minimum fuss . Backwards compatibility is a cruel mistress .

When a country is spending more money than it is taking in it can do one of three things , none of the very nice . 1 . It can decide to * * default * * and not pay it 's debt . This is possibly the worst answer since it means no one will trust the country 's debt and their credit rating will drop . Eurozone countries really ca n't do this because one default will hurt all countries on the Euro . 2 . It can * * inflate * * it 's money by printing too much money . This makes debt easier to pay but means that the savings of middle class families will be worth less . Eurozone countries ca n't do this either because all money printing need all the countries to agree and healthy countries like Germany do n't want to lose their savings because Greece and Spain ca n't pay their bills . 3 . It can implement * * austerity * * , dramatic and sudden cuts to spending with sudden increases to taxes . This is painful because it means less medical attention , less unemployment assistance , less old age pension etc . It also slows down the economy because average people have less money to spend because retired people and unemployed people have less money and taxes are higher . The problem is that the slow down in the economy usually means that any savings the government gets is lost because a slower economy means less taxable income , it is a bad and painful way to solve debt but it is the only one a member of the Euro can do by themselves . So austerity is very unpopular , not super effective but it is the only thing that can be done alone . This means these countries still generally need assistance , the bailouts being given to countries like Greece and Spain are to help cover what austerity alone ca n't . But because it is being paid for by healthy countries the bailout usually comes with conditions including austerity . Think how unpopular wall street bailouts were , and that was the US bailing out its own citizens and its own banks . Imagine if we had to bail out Mexico because Mexico is spending too much money on itself . Austerity is the only way Germany and others are willing to give money to preserve the Euro .

Here we have a cardboard box , about the size of a shoebox , filled with coins . Not a ton of them , just enough so that the all lay flat on the bottom of the box . There are three possible states for that box to be in : all coins heads - side - up , all coins heads - side - down , and the other thing where some of the coins are heads - up and some are heads - down . Imagine the box starts out in the all - coins - up state . We put the lid on and shake it . Without opening the lid , what state do you expect the box to be in ? The answer is obvious : Some of the coins will be heads - up and some will be heads - down . Why ? Because the all - heads - up and all - heads - down states correspond to * exactly one arrangement of coins * each , while there are * many * arrangements of coins that correspond to the some - up - some - down state . The " all - up , all - down , some - of - both " states are what we call * macrostates . * They 're the states we care about , the ones we can easily observe . The individual position and heads - up - or - down - ness of all the coins comprises what we call a * microstate . * It 's a state that is normally invisible to us , hidden from view , either because we just do n't care about that much detail , or because that much detail is practically impossible for us to measure . Entropy is , in a sense , how many microstates correspond to a particular macrostate . In this example , the all - heads - up and all - heads - down macrostates each correspond to just a single microstate ; that 's a very low - entropy condition . But the some - of - each macrostate corresponds to * many * microstates , making that a high - entropy condition . When we started out , all the coins were heads - side - up , but when we put the lid on the box and shook it , the system moved from a low - entropy state to a high - entropy state . In nature , systems always tend to move from low - entropy to high - entropy states . In the most abstract sense , this is just because of pure dumb luck : There are more combinations of coins that add up to " some of each " than either " all up " or " all down , " so * pure random chance * dictates that we 're far more likely to go from the all - up state to the some - of - each state than the other way around … and furthermore , that as we continue to shake the box , we 're far more likely to * stay * in the some - of - each state , because the odds against getting all the coins to land heads - side - up are enormous . In reality , this use of pure - dumb - luck - based statistics to describe complex systems is a mathematical approximation . After all , things like the motions of molecules in a bathtub of water are n't really random . They 're actually the product of a * huge * number of very simple interactions … but that 's the thing . When you take something that 's fundamentally simple but that becomes vastly complex because of sheer scale , that thing tends to behave very much like a purely random system governed by dumb luck . So it turns out those dumb - luck - based statistical approximations are actually incredibly useful and predictive . So basically , entropy can be thought of as a way of quantifying just how likely or unlikely it is that a complex system will evolve in a particular way . If the evolution you 're imagining is from a low - entropy state to a high - entropy state , in general that 's pretty likely . If it 's the other way around , from a high - entropy state to a low - entropy state , then in general that 's probably not going to happen . The more complex the system you 're thinking about , the better statistical methods tend to be for predicting the evolution of that system over time .

In computer science, the time complexity is the computational complexity that describes the amount of computer time it takes to run an algorithm. Time complexity is commonly estimated by counting the number of elementary operations performed by the algorithm, supposing that each elementary operation takes a fixed amount of time to perform. Thus, the amount of time taken and the number of elementary operations performed by the algorithm are taken to be related by a constant factor.
Since an algorithm's running time may vary among different inputs of the same size, one commonly considers the worst-case time complexity, which is the maximum amount of time required for inputs of a given size. Less common, and usually specified explicitly, is the average-case complexity, which is the average of the time taken on inputs of a given size (this makes sense because there are only a finite number of possible inputs of a given size). In both cases, the time complexity is generally expressed as a function of the size of the input.: 226  Since this function is generally difficult to compute exactly, and the running time for small inputs is usually not consequential, one commonly focuses on the behavior of the complexity when the input size increases—that is, the asymptotic behavior of the complexity. There is no benefit . Helium-3 is the best fuel source for fusion power , but fusion power is n't anywhere near being viable nor is there any expectation that it ever will be . The whole mining Helium-3 on the moon thing is a science fiction plot device and nothing more . The dirt on the moon contains a few parts per billion of Helium-3 , which is a lot more than you can find in dirt on the Earth . But its still almost nothing . Even in a science fiction fantasy land where industrial scale mining on the moon was possible you would expend far more energy trying to mine that Helium than you could ever get back from it . Right now on Earth we mine Helium as a byproduct of natural gas extraction , and most commercial Helium production comes from a few natural gas fields in Texas that contain ~7 % Helium by volume . Most of that Helium is Helium-4 , but those fields still contain several orders of magnitude more Helium-3 per volume than the dirt on the moon does . If there was ever demand for Helium-3 , we would just purify it from existing Helium production on Earth . The reason you 're hearing about it in the news is because India claimed it plans to build a Helium-3 mine on the moon by 2030 . But just like the $ 4 smartphone and the Indian male contraceptive thing that was going around on Reddit a few weeks ago this claim is bogus . Right now India is in the middle of a nationalistic fervor and is churning out dozens of stories about wondrous scientific advances that are going to turn India into the world 's greatest super power . But thus far every single one of those stories has been nothing more than the Indian press playing to that nationalistic climate . Right now India 's space program is at about the same point that the US space program was in the early 60 's and this story is nothing more than the Indian government trying to generate good press for itself . In short - anytime you hear about some wondrous new invention or program coming out of India you should take it with a gigantic grain of salt . There is no serious plan to mine Helium on the moon and its doubtful that there ever will be .

One of the biggest differences is the amount of random access memory ( RAM ) that is accessible to the program . RAM is the place where your programs does its work while your hard disk is where it stores your files and where the program you are using temporarily puts stuff when all available RAM is full . Think of the RAM in your computer as your desk while the hard disk on your computer as your filing cabinet . If you have a giant desk ( more RAM ) , then you can take out all of the files you need and lay them all out for easy access so that you can see it all and get your work done . If you had a small desk that only fits one file but you need many files to do your work , you can only open up one file , do some work , put the file back and get another file . You still get your work done , but you are able to get more complex work done more effectively with a large desk . Extending that analogy , if you have a giant map that you have to unfold , you can do it on a large desk , but you ca n't on a small desk . If we use your photoshop example , the 64 bit version can give you a much larger desk ( assuming your computer physically HAS lots of RAM ) to lay out your giant map on . This lets you see larger maps ( edit larger images ) more efficiently .

This question has been asked and answered before. Financially, owning a car will be more economical than leasing one in most cases. The reason for this is that leasing arrangements are designed to make a profit for the leasing company over and above the value of the car. A leasing company that does not profit off their customers will not be in business for long. This is a zero-sum game and the leasing customer is the loser. The lion's share of the customer losses are in maintenance and in the event of an accident or other damage. In both cases, leasing arrangements are designed to make a large profit for the owner. The average customer assumes they will never get into an accident and they underestimate the losses they will take on the maintenance. For example, if both oxygen sensors need to be replaced and it would have cost you $800 to replace them yourself, but the leasing company charges you $1200, then BOOM! you just lost $400. If the car is totaled, the customer will lose many thousands of dollars. Leasing contracts are designed to make money for the owner, not the customer. Another way leasing agents make money is on "required maintenance". Most leasing contracts require the leasor to perform "required" maintenance, oil changes, tire rotations, etc. Also, with newer cars manufacturers recalls are common. Those are required as well. Nearly nobody does this maintenance correctly. This gives the agent the excuse to charge the customer thousands of dollars when the vehicle is returned. Bills of $4000 to $6000 on a 3 year lease for failure to perform required maintenance are common. Its items like this that allow the leasing agent to get a profit on what looks like a "good deal" when the customer walked in the door 3 years previously. The advantage of leasing is that it costs less up front and it is more convenient to switch to a different car because you don't have to sell the car.

Lifelong Londoner here . I 'll try to explain . First , you have one outright mistake in your question , and a couple of points of confusion . The outright mistake is this : " Camden is a borough in the city of Westminster " . No , it is n't . So let 's try to clarify . " Greater London " is a geographic and political entity . It 's approximately 600 square miles in size and has a population of around 8.5 million people . Certain functions , such as transport and many development and economic issues , are considered important enough that there is an elected mayor for the whole of Greater London ( known , unsurprisingly , as the Mayor of London , but we 'll come back to this ) , and an elected body , the Greater London Assembly , which between them set policies . The Mayor and GLA also provide the budget for the police and fire service in London , and have a say in their running . To provide more local representation and deal with less London - wide issues , Greater London is then subdivided into 33 boroughs . One of these boroughs is the London Borough of Camden , another is the London Borough of the City of Westminster ( we 'll come to the reason for it having " city " in its name later ) . But Camden and Westminster are completely separate entities ; apart from the fact that they 're both one of the 33 boroughs they have nothing else in common . Most of the boroughs have an official name of " The London Borough of * whatever * " but a couple are called " The City of * whatever * " . This is because the history of human settlement in the London area dates way , way , back , long before the establishment of Greater London . For example the city of Westminster grew up from about the 10th century , so as the area that was later called London grew it was eventually swallowed up . The borough retains the name of what was once a separate , independent city , however . And then there 's the borough called " The City of London " . This one is special in many ways . The City of London ( the word " City " is * * always * * spelt with a capital in this context ) is tiny . It 's only about one square mile in size . It 's essentially the area that once sat within the old Roman walls of their city of * Londinium * , and today it 's completely dominated by being the headquarters of many financial companies -- so much so that in the UK simply saying " The City " is a shorthand way of saying " financial services companies " , just as in the US saying " Wall Street " has the same connotation . Despite being a borough of Greater London , the City of London has many special privileges and rights , which have been laid down over about a thousand years worth of legislation . In particular it elects its own mayor , known as " The Lord Mayor of London " although this is now a ceremonial position rather than one with any specific power . The City has its own separate police force from the rest of Greater London , has many of its own laws , and ( somewhat controversially ) positions on the City of London council are voted for by the corporations that are based in the City , rather than the ( very few ) people who live there , as happens in every other UK election .

An integrated circuit or monolithic integrated circuit (also referred to as an IC, a chip, or a microchip) is a set of electronic circuits on one small flat piece (or "chip") of semiconductor material, usually silicon. Large numbers of tiny MOSFETs (metal–oxide–semiconductor field-effect transistors) integrate into a small chip. This results in circuits that are orders of magnitude smaller, faster, and less expensive than those constructed of discrete electronic components. The IC's mass production capability, reliability, and building-block approach to integrated circuit design has ensured the rapid adoption of standardized ICs in place of designs using discrete transistors. ICs are now used in virtually all electronic equipment and have revolutionized the world of electronics. Computers, mobile phones and other home appliances are now inextricable parts of the structure of modern societies, made possible by the small size and low cost of ICs such as modern computer processors and microcontrollers.
Very-large-scale integration was made practical by technological advancements in metal–oxide–silicon (MOS) semiconductor device fabrication. Since their origins in the 1960s, the size, speed, and capacity of chips have progressed enormously, driven by technical advances that fit more and more MOS transistors on chips of the same size – a modern chip may have many billions of MOS transistors in an area the size of a human fingernail. These advances, roughly following Moore's law, make the computer chips of today possess millions of times the capacity and thousands of times the speed of the computer chips of the early 1970s.
ICs have two main advantages over discrete circuits: cost and performance. The cost is low because the chips, with all their components, are printed as a unit by photolithography rather than being constructed one transistor at a time. Furthermore, packaged ICs use much less material than discrete circuits. Performance is high because the IC's components switch quickly and consume comparatively little power because of their small size and proximity. The main disadvantage of ICs is the high cost of designing them and fabricating the required photomasks. This high initial cost means ICs are only commercially viable when high production volumes are anticipated.
