A computer program takes in some data ( input ) and puts out some data ( output ) . And a program itself is a piece of data . So one program can be input to another program . ( Also , a program can * output * a program ; that 's what a compiler does . ) Some programs run forever ( on particular input ) , and some programs stop . For instance , here is a program in BASIC that runs forever : 10 PRINT " Hello " 20 GOTO 10 Here is a program that stops : 10 PRINT " Hello " 20 PRINT " Ok , I 'm done . " And here is a program that will run forever or stop depending on the input : 10 INPUT A$ 20 IF A$ = " STOP " THEN GOTO 50 30 PRINT " Running forever ! " 40 GOTO 30 50 PRINT " Stoppingly stopping ! " The halting problem asks , can we make a program that will look at another program and a piece of input , and tell whether that program will stop , given that input ? Let 's use some math . We 'll say that X(Y ) means " run program X on input Y " . And if a program takes two inputs Y and Z , we 'll write it as X(Y , Z ) . So , the halting problem is : Can we write a program H(P , I ) that will take a program P and a piece of input I , and tell whether P(I ) runs forever or stops ? Well , suppose we could . Suppose we wrote a program H that did just that ! It would output " STOP " or " FOREVER " depending on whether P(I ) would stop or run forever . Now , we could write a new program G(X ) . G takes program X as input , and what G does is to run H(X , X ) to find out whether X(X ) stops or runs forever . Then G(X ) decides what * it * should do , based on what H(X , X ) said . If H(X , X ) says " STOP " , then G(X ) runs forever , just like the BASIC program above . And if H(X , X ) says " FOREVER " , then G(X ) stops . Now we run G(G ) . G(G ) runs H(G , G ) which asks whether G(G ) stops . If H(G , G ) says it stops , then G(G ) decides to run forever ­ — which means H(G , G ) is buggy ! And if H(G , G ) says that G(G ) runs forever , then G(G ) will decide to stop . So again , H has a bug ; it gives the wrong answer . Oops ! No matter what H does , it can not have the right answer ! H is an impossible program . It 's not possible to write a program that perfectly tells whether other programs stop or run forever . Now , there are a lot of programs that we * can * tell if they stop or run forever . For instance , a program with a loop back to the beginning — like the first one above — obviously runs forever . And a program with no loops or GOTOs in it — like the second one above — certainly does n't run forever . We can also prove that a lot of other programs with loops will eventually stop . And we can write programs that will prove this for us . But there 's no way to write a program that will correctly answer this question for * every possible * program , because we can always come up with a tricky program like G that disproves it .

Why is this downvoted ? We 're at ELI5 here , and this is actually a very relevant question . People have been struggling with it since the dawn of civilization , and it 's unlikely we 'll ever find a clear - cut answer . It was [ Ferdinand de Saussure ] ( URL_2 ) who first made clear that there is no " why " as to the naming of the objects and concepts of the world around us : he pointed out that the labels ( i.e. , words ) we attach to those concepts are entirely arbitrary . Of course , words have an * [ etymology ] ( URL_0 ) * , but that only shows the development of the word and its usage during the history of its ( known ) existence . It does not answer the question why people decided to refer to the concept " TREE " with the sound * tree * , or * arbre * ( French ) , or * Baum * ( German ) etc . The answer is as simple as it is disappointing : we do n't know why things are called the way they 're called . There is an important exception to this principle , though : words that imitate sounds ( known as [ onomatopoeia ] ( URL_3 ) ) or movement and such . With those words we can say with certainty * why * a cuckoo is called a cuckoo : because that 's the sound it makes . I hope this addresses your question a little bit , and shows you that it was n't as stupid a question as some here seem to think . Of course , I gave you the ELI5 explanation ; reality is always more complex , but this is the gist of it . If you 're interested in learning more , I can highly recommend Guy Deutscher 's * [ The Unfolding of Language ] ( URL_1 ) * , an excellent and very readable layman 's introduction in how language ( may have ) evolved . For all else , there is /r / linguistics !

1 . Access to money . Banks , because they have so much liquidity , are the primary lenders in a society . Without banks , people ( who want to buy homes , cars , go to school etc .. ) and small businesses ( who want to buy capital ) would have a much harder time . 2 . Fractional reserve banking allows for money creation- this is a tad more complex and the benefits are challenged by more than a few on the relatively far left and right . Way back when , the first bankers were the gold smiths- people entrusted their gold to them because they had the best security for their gold . Maybe they 'd pay a bit for the service . Let 's say one such gold smith had an average of100 lbs of other people 's gold in his vault . Now , this number changes from day to day as people withdraw and deposit- but he notices that never has he had less than 60 lbs . Essentially , this means he only needs to keep 40 lbs of gold on hand- unless something exceptional happens , he 'll be able to give everybody who wants to make a withdrawal their gold . This means he can loan out the remaining 60 , which leads to the benefit described above . But it goes a bit further than that- Let 's say you deposit 10 lbs of gold , and the banker puts 40 % of that aside and loans out the remaining 6 lbs to me . It may not seem like it , but that just created money- sure , there are only 10 lbs of gold in real life , and yet I have 10 lbs of gold and I have 6 , for a total of 16 lbs .

Well there are a few angles we could look at this from . The first one could look at it from the perspective of the idea of risk compensation . People who believe they are in a position of safety tend to behave more risky and vice versa . In college , heavy drinking is common ( albeit not as common as portrayed in some media ) and you tend to learn how to " drink safely " . With the knowledge of proper drinking acquired through years of trial and error , people become more willing to take the risk that comes with drinking under the assumption that they 've had good experiences in the past and thus ca n't have problems with it In that same line of reasoning , they may have developed a taste for alcoholic beverages or an increased tolerance while in college , allowing them to drink with regularity and not suffer adverse consequence . It could be a financial thing . People at the higher end of the social scale tend to have more expendable income and for many people alcohol is considered a luxury . Having the means to be able to drink more regularly allows for the above two prescribed scenarios . There have been a few studies that have shown that money only affects your happiness up to a certain point . Once you have the means to provide for yourself comfortably , money 's relation to happiness is actually pretty minimal as you move up the pay scale . So by this logic , depression and the alcohol dependence that can frequently accompany it , would be equivocal between the wealthy and the population at large . People like drinking and so having the means is usually the only barrier but the study in question does n't say they drink more , it only says a higher percentage of the wealthy drink and that could be caused by any of the things I 've mentioned .

There 's a lot of good information here but I think maybe they 're giving you details you do n't care about . The letters at the end of the 802.11 are what " version " of WiFi you have . Right now AC is the most recent version but N is still very common too . Newer version routers will still be able to talk to older version devices ( for example , If you have an AC router it will still be able to talk to your N laptop ) . Newer versions will typically be faster than older versions . When two different versions are talking to each other the speed will be whatever the slowest device is . ( For example , if an AC router is talking to an N laptop , then the connection will be running at N speeds since that 's the slowest device ) . For most of WiFi 's history the frequencies ( a.k.a . channels ) have been in the 2.4GHz band . However , newer WiFi routers are also able to transmit in the 5GHz band . 5GHz is a little faster and often less congested but the range suffers a little bit since the signal ca n't penetrate obstacles quite as easily . The difference usually is n't noticeable unless you have a big house . It also must be noted that there 's no backwards compatibility when it comes to the frequencies - if you want to use 5GHz then both your router AND your end device have to support 5GHz . If either of them lacks 5GHz support then both of them will default to 2.4GHz instead . Do n't worry about overlapping channels and finding unused frequencies and all that - modern WiFi systems are smart enough to do all that for you automatically . TL;DR - Pick the highest letter you can find ( AC is the newest ) , use 5GHz if you can ( both your router AND your end device have to support it ) , and do n't worry about specific channels because your router will handle that part for you automatically .

By around 1910 the use of radio in the USA was becoming common enough that Congress passed its first laws to regulate its use . Tellingly , this was the " Wireless Ship Act of 1910 , " as naval communications was the most critical purpose of radio technology , and the primary purpose of this act was to require ships of certain size to have wireless sets for emergency and other operational purposes . The Radio Act of 1912 ( a response to the sinking of the Titanic ) bolstered the above law , and began the practice of licensing individual transmitters , assigning individuals or companies responsibilities based on that license , limiting frequencies and power levels and other technical aspects , and assigning unique call signs . But what should a call sign look like ? Well , a series of international commissions on radio ( referred to at the time as " International Radiotelegraphic Conferences " ) developed an international system of call signs , granting each nation a block of them . The standard at the time was usually 3 letters , A - Z - a total of 17,576 possible stations , although longer call signs existed ( or would shortly ) . The USA had already been using call signs starting with N ( common starting letter for US Navy stations ) , so it got the assignement of every radio station starting with N and a subset of stations starting with AA through AL ( for America , presumably , although they had to share about half the possible A - stations ) . Some of the IRC ( later ITU ) assignments made sense - Russia got R , France F , Italy I , Great Britain G. To allow for the fact some nations needed a lot of call signs due to their population and level of technical development , some nations got letters that were pretty much randomly assigned : China got B , Great Britain got M in addition to G , and the USA got W and K along with N. Various other nations received only portions of blocks . ( EDIT : These are really post - WWI assignments ; the ELI5 is complicated enough without explaining how the US 's call sign blocks changed over decades of IRC / ITU agreements . ) So , that 's US stations available to assign as of ~~1912~~ 1920 - some of the A block ( America ) , all the N block ( Navy ) and W and K ( because nobody else wanted those letters badly enough to fight for them ) . The US decided to reserve AA - AL and N for government use , leaving K and W for non - government licenses . ~~The next step is obvious , right ? Give W to the Western USA and keep K for the Eastern USA , ( K is kinda close to E ... ).~~ ~~Nope ! No such rule existed ; K - stations and W - stations were granted all over the USA , which is why you have WBZ Boston ( licensed 1921 ) and KJR Seattle ( licensed 1922 ) and various other outliers.~~ EDIT : W and K stations were initially not geographically linked , with the Eastern US having a fair mix of both , but around 1912 ( ? ) it was decided to split them , with K in the West and W in the East . The dividing line was initially the Western border of the Dakotas , Nebraska , Kansas , Oklahoma , and Texas . This shifted to the Mississippi River in 1923 . For this reason , you have still - existing stations like KYW Pittsburgh ( licensed 1921 ) and WKY Oklahoma City ( licensed 1922 ) . Regulations up to that point had focused on radio as a 2 - way communications tool , but the development of practical microphones , speakers , and home radio receivers right around 1920 suddenly created a demand for a totally new type of radio - broadcast radio - where a single ( very powerful ) station transmitted news , entertainment , and other information , many hours a day , many days a week . Existing US regulations were n't prepared for hundreds of such transmitters popping up all over the place in the space of a few years , and needed a lot more resources and better organization to handle the fact a device designed for transmitting short messages between places was suddenly a font for popular media . By 1922 , 3 - letter K and W call signs had been more or less used up , and future broadcast licenses were 4 letters , and often longer for amateur and other - purposed stations . The Radio Act of 1927 generated the Federal Radio Commission , which FINALLY ~~decided on~~ EDIT : made law the ~~W / K~~ K / W West / East split in 1928 to help simplify the process of broadcast call sign assignments . The Communications Act of 1934 replaced this with the FCC , which upheld the same rule . Additional links , much more detailed and accurate than the above synopsis : URL_2 URL_1 URL_0

Here is the short blurb from Wikipedia : > Objectivism 's central tenets are that reality exists independently of consciousness , that human beings have direct contact with reality through sense perception , that one can attain objective knowledge from perception through the process of concept formation and inductive logic , that the proper moral purpose of one 's life is the pursuit of one 's own happiness ( rational self - interest ) , that the only social system consistent with this morality is one that displays full respect for individual rights embodied in laissez - faire capitalism , and that the role of art in human life is to transform humans ' metaphysical ideas by selective reproduction of reality into a physical form — a work of art — that one can comprehend and to which one can respond emotionally . Basically : - There is an objective reality independent of what you perceive . For example , an apple has the color red , even if you are color blind and can not see red . - You can use your senses to understand this reality , but since your senses may have limitations ( our color blind person above ) you sometimes have to fill in the gaps those limitations produce with logic - The ultimate goal of any being is to make itself happy - You can not force anyone to do anything against their will , because that would force unhappiness on them ( even if it makes a third party happier in the process ) .

Yes airspace has speed limits in some fashion or another but whether it has hard speed limits is another matter . Firstly anything travelling through the air in our atmosphere has a physics based speed limit . That means you can only go so fast through the air before the air that your aircraft is colliding with in front of you heats up to the point of vaporising you ( Because air has mass ) . This is the same reason spaceships returning from space heat up when they encounter the atmosphere and require heat shields . However that speed is pretty damn phenomenal . There 's no manned aircraft around today capable of achieving it . The other kind of speed limit is one that is imposed on us by our own restrictions , most usually in the form of air traffic control to insure aircraft stay safely away from each other in CONTROLLED airspace . However they are not the same kind of speed limit as they are imposed on roads . They work along a more voluntarily basis . An air traffic controller will always give a plane an appropriate speed for the type of aircraft it is and to balance its position in traffic , he will also take in other factors such as weather . For example an air traffic controller will never give a plane a speed that it is not capable of achieving or a speed that is too slow ( Otherwise it might stall ) . If an air traffic controller tells a plane to maintain 300 knots , he 's not actually saying " You MUST maintain this speed " . He 's asking the aircraft to maintain the speed if it 's able to do so , and if not then to inform him / her that they can not do so and why . He will then use this information to route the aircraft more appropriately . There 's all kinds of reasons why the plane might not be able to maintain that speed and while the pilot will do his very best to maintain the speed he has been told to do so ( And 99.9 % of the time this is the case ) , sometimes you just ca nt . Flying a plane is just not like driving a car , there 's so many other factors to take account of . For example a massive tailwind can be pushing your speed up by an amount of knots and balancing the throttle to reduce the airspeed could be counter productive . Height to some extent has some of the same flexibility but much less so . Direction however is pretty much unarguable . Unless your aircraft is in difficulty or a situation develops that forces you to change direction there 's no excuse in the world for not maintaining the correct heading . But here 's something most people do n't know . Domestic airspace is not heavily controlled . And it surprises most people . What this means is that I can jump in my aircraft ( I 'm in the UK ) , take off from my local airfield , fly somewhere and land . At no point would I be required to file a flight plan , and provided I work within certain limits , I could do so at whatever height and speed I liked provided my aircraft was capable of it . The only interaction with air traffic control would be traffic advisory ( I.e. telling them I 'm in the area and what my intentions are , and them giving me information about traffic that s in my area ) . This is generally called uncontrolled airspace and the great majority of the UK domestic airspace is made up of this . It 's not until you enter controlled airspace that you have to start obeying air traffic controllers orders . Source : Am pilot ( Private pilots license ) .

* * A 2D TRIANGLE * * Start with flat 2D geometry : Put 2 points , A and B , on a piece of paper , and draw a straight line between them , and measure length of line from A to B. Next , add a 3rd point , C , anywhere on the paper , and connect that to points A and B to make a triangle . Can you make the line from A to C to B as short as the line from A to B ? The only way to do that is to put C on the line between A and B. At that point , though , it ceases to be a triangle , and is really just the line from A to B with another mark on it . * * DEFINING " GREAT CIRCLE " * * Now , let 's try the same experiment on a 3D sphere . When working in 2D , we only needed 2 starting points . With 3D , we need 3 starting points . If point A is Sydney , point B is Johannesburg ( A and B could be any other two different points on the sphere , as well ) , and point C is the center of the sphere ( Hey ! C for center ! That works out well ! ) , you can imagine 1 flat surface going through each of those points , dividing the sphere into 2 parts . Further , because this flat surface goes through the center of the sphere , you also know that the flat surface is dividing the Earth exactly in half . In other words , if you took the sphere apart where its cut by that flat surface , the edge would not only be circular , but it would be the largest possible circle you could make with that sphere . It 's the greatest possible circle size , or " great circle " for short . The shortest distance between any two points on that circle , is along that great circle itself . * * SHORTEST POSSIBLE DISTANCE PROOF * * Want proof ? OK , we 're going to do that triangle experiment again , but this time on the surface of the sphere ! Points A ( Sydney ) and B ( Johannesburg ) are already on the great circle , of course , because that 's how we started . Choose a point D that 's on the surface of the sphere , but not on the great circle . Points A , B , and D now form a spherical triangle ( a triangle on the surface of a sphere ) . Just like in the flat triangle version , the only way to get the line from A to D to B to be as short as the line from A to B is to move D so that it 's on the line from A to B. There 's your proof that the shortest distance along the surface of a sphere between any two points on that surface is along the great circle ! * * BONUS TRIVIA * * Planes flying from San Francisco to Tokyo fly a great circle path , but planes flying from Tokyo to San Francisco do not . Planes flying from Tokyo to San Francisco can save fuel by taking advantage of the jet stream over the Pacific .

It 's a thing from the past , but companies are still milking it . Essentially , back in the day phone lines where actual copper all the way . Copper run from your house to your local exchange , and from there nationwide . International calls where part copper , part radio , radio being either land - to - land or land - satellite - land . The entire network did n't belong to the same operator , and even if it did , he still had increased costs for a longer call . Satellite bandwidth is insanely expensive . Nowdays , it 's all IP . Even companies that sell you regular landlines have their backbones in IP , and their connection to other carriers is over IP too . It 's fiber all the way , and a phone call uses relatively low bandwidth , so the physical cost of actually calling someone on the other side of the globe is negligible , most of their cost is in the last mile and backbone . But of course , companies are still milking a thing of the past , so , when you want to buy minutes from another exchange , that exchange will sell you minutes at a different price than it sells them locally , even if that provider is the local exchange for that particular destination . So , say , the phone company in Somewhere , Some country will sell minutes at 0.01 to their local users in Somewhere , or will even offer unlimited plans , but when Someotherplace , Someothercountry wants to interconnect with them , Somewhere will sell ( and buy ) those same minutes at a higher price , when actually their cost of selling them to Someotherplace is lower , since it 's a telco to telco interconnection , which most of the time is just a SIP account , and the maintenance and setup costs of that trunk is lower than a single last mile for a single local customer .

While I 'm not an expert on power vs. displacement / cylinders , I can speak to pieces of this puzzle . They do actually make a 3.0 liter V12 engine which is in F1 race cars , so they do exist . And you have large displacement engine with 2 cylinders as well , check out a car call the ' Morgan . ' You have to consider a few things when looking at these kinds of engines ( again , I 'm sure more knowledgable people will add to this list such as power / weight ratios and whatnot ): * Does it fit in an engine bay ? * Will it run smoothly ? * What 's the durability / longevity going to look like ? * How many moving parts are there going to be ? First off , a huge V - twin or 3 cylinder engine would probably make lots of power , but they are harder to fit in an engine bay , and will vibrate more and require a massive rotating mass or flywheel to keep the the thing running . The implications of this setup would be a decent amount of torque , but the acceleration would be hindered simply from trying to rotate that flywheel as well . By the same token a small displacement V12 would run very smoothly and have a neat sound , but you 'd have a hard time fitting into a small engine bay . The other issue with a V12 is the shear number of moving internal parts . This would possible cause some reliability issues and cost a ton to repair . All in all , it 's a balance of what can be manufactured cheaply , balance ride comfort ( smooth running ) and reliability . That 's why you find 2.0 liter engines as a 4 cylinder , 3.0 liter engines as a 6 cylinder , and 4.0 liter engines as an 8 cylinder . You 're balancing all of these things .

1 . Spending . NASA has triple the budget of the ESA - and US spending is down significantly under the Obama Administration . Point being : NASA spends more , and it always has , and it 's not close . 2 . Efficiency . The US program has long been derided for being wasteful , but anyone who things the US is wasteful should take a look at the ESA . Programs must be , by treaty , divided among countries such that each country 's aviation industry gets a piece of the pie . The ESA really functions like a subsidy for big European Aerospace companies . 3 . Leadership . NASA is under the direction of one person who takes his orders from the President of the United States . Congress must approve some of those orders , but generally speaking there is one leader whose vision gets legislative approval . The ESA requires approval from all the major members of the ESA to do anything significant - making big projects and direction changes difficult to handle . 4 . Priorities . Europe is mostly socialist ( in the political sense , not the ideological one ) and for that reason they are not so opposed to high tax rates , but they tend to divert extra money into social programs . In the US , money tends to go into R & D programs ( like NASA , or much of the defense spending ) at a far higher rate than social programs . It 's not that Europeans do n't care about science , it 's that the US does n't care about social programs . ( And it shows in our national rankings of things like quality of life and education ) .

Americans seem to love freedom so much , and have such a focus on our flag , because it represents a great deal about our shared cultural heritage . Many other nations around the world have many hundreds or thousands of years of shared culture and history binding them together . They have traditions that everyone in the country can gather around and connect with . It has n't always been that way for the US , and still largely is n't . Many people in America today seem to forget that our country is a nation of immigrants . Peoples from all over the world have come to this country , have brought their own cultures and traditions with them , and have largely found that America is a place where they can hold onto what defined them in their home countries , while still becoming " American , " too . So there 's an element of American society where we have this general culture defined by things like the flag and its colors , or like those national American holidays anyone of any religion can celebrate . The flag , then , becomes the thing that Americans can share , even if two given Americans do n't sound the same , look the same , think the same , eat the same , or lead lives that look anything alike . It 's not about our flag being better or in some way more a symbol of freedom than any other nation 's . It 's that the flag , and the freedom it supposedly represents , is what we 've built our nation 's mythology on .

The short and unexciting answer , is that we ca n't really answer that question . Presumably the human brain would have evolved to represent these somehow , but it has n't , so we can only ' pretend ' to see them by essentially using technology to shift them into the visible spectrum . But some make - believe dust added and ... If you can see radiowaves , then astronomical objects , your radio , your wifi router , technological stuff in general , will put out lots of ' light ' . If you could see microwaves , well , the cosmic microwave radiation that fills the universe would presumably fill your vision with light everywhere , all the time , forever . I 'd find it kind of annoying honestly . It definitely would n't help you see very well . If you could see infrared , well , you 'd see akin to how infrared cameras present things , presumably . Blobs of ' light ' dependent upon temperature . Potentially useful in some respects , problematic in others . You could find a dropped cigarette pretty easily . Your girlfriend 's face would be a smear of light though . Ultraviolet light is given off by stars , televisions , certain lights , you 'd have essentially some overlap with things that already light up for you . If you could percieve x - rays , you 'd see dim shadows of the objects they pass through , like your neighbor 's skeleton . This of course would make seeing things as we do now kind of difficult . You would n't see anything different in the night sky from that though , as the atmosphere blocks it out . Gamma rays would be visible coming from radioactive stuff , and from the night sky . If intense enough , I suppose they would pierce through solid objects near you . In short , it 'd be a real pain in the ass , because you 'd be overwhelmed with ' light ' that would not correspond to solid objects in your vicinity particularly conveniently .

Not a full lawyer , so do n't hold me 100 % to this . First , I advise be polite . It 's not a rule , but being hostile will almost never end well and being polite often speeds things up . The vast vast majority of cops are not like the few you hear of on r / politics breaking the rules , they are doing their job and respecting that helps move things along ( and frankly they are often less inclined to search a cooperating person ) . Anything in plain view , a cop may look at . Anyplace you have a " reasonable expectation of privacy " meaning your home with the blinds closed , a fully zipped backpack , a purse etc can be searched only with your consent or with probable cause . If you consent they may act on anything they find . Others can not give consent on your behalf , so for example you could not let cops in to your roommate 's bedroom or car . If a cop claims probable cause , ask what the probable cause is , it may come up as a defense if it is a shaky reasoning . Refusing to cooperate is not probable cause , but sniffing dogs indicating your bag ( for example ) is considered probable cause . If a cop says they have a warrant , ask to see the warrant and what it covers . A warrant is specific about where a cop may look and what they are looking for . In either of these cases the cops do not need your permission to conduct a search . If during the course of a legal search they see something in plain view not covered by the warrant they may act on it . If it is something that may be destroyed such as documents or drugs they may seize them . If you are being questioned , ask if you are free to go . If they say you may not go you are considered in police custody and therefore have the right to an attorney . If you ask for an attorney the cops may not do or say anything to gather information from you without a lawyer present . If cops break any of these rules to gather information , that information may never be used in court . Not only that , but if information gathered illegally leads to other information all that information is thrown out as well . Such information is considered " fruit from the poison tree " and makes ( most ) cops very careful about how they gather information because it can lead to guilty men walking free if handled wrong .

Many answers to each of these questions Why do people make them ? - fun - learn programing - profit - cause inconvenience \ damage to the operating system - laughs - to be " cool " What benefits do they get , if any ? - fun - profit ( governments do develop virus 's but more commonly they also have a trojan component ) - programming skill - learn something new - feel cool - stolen passwords - stolen credit card numbers \ personal data - there has also been virus 's made that fix your computer How do they work ? - Most virus 's today try and replicate as much as possible using any way they can to get from one computer to the next - infected JPEG\exe files are common - a famous example : URL_1 using a file with the name of good looking people to click on it - they can either exploit a failure in software design that alows them to spread or trick users . - another good example is when people download a key generator for a popular piece of software , quite often a person will join a virus to the generator and re upload it to the internet for victim 's to download . ----- What Is a Virus ? A computer virus attaches itself to a program or file enabling it to spread from one computer to another , leaving infections as it travels . Like a human virus , a computer virus can range in severity : some may cause only mildly annoying effects while others can damage your hardware , software or files . Almost all viruses are attached to an executable file , which means the virus may exist on your computer but it actually can not infect your computer unless you run or open the malicious program . It is important to note that a virus can not be spread without a human action , ( such as running an infected program ) to keep it going . Because a virus is spread by human action people will unknowingly continue the spread of a computer virus by sharing infecting files or sending emails with viruses as attachments in the email . What Is a Worm ? Sponsored Is your network ready for the cloud ? Find out : read “ Five Reasons Classic Ethernet Switches Wo n't Support the Cloud ” and learn how to examine your network ’s strength and eliminate any weak points . A worm is similar to a virus by design and is considered to be a sub - class of a virus . Worms spread from computer to computer , but unlike a virus , it has the capability to travel without any human action . A worm takes advantage of file or information transport features on your system , which is what allows it to travel unaided . The biggest danger with a worm is its capability to replicate itself on your system , so rather than your computer sending out a single worm , it could send out hundreds or thousands of copies of itself , creating a huge devastating effect . One example would be for a worm to send a copy of itself to everyone listed in your e - mail address book . Then , the worm replicates and sends itself out to everyone listed in each of the receiver 's address book , and the manifest continues on down the line . Due to the copying nature of a worm and its capability to travel across networks the end result in most cases is that the worm consumes too much system memory ( or network bandwidth ) , causing Web servers , network servers and individual computers to stop responding . In recent worm attacks such as the much - talked - about Blaster Worm , the worm has been designed to tunnel into your system and allow malicious users to control your computer remotely . What Is a Trojan horse ? A Trojan Horse is full of as much trickery as the mythological Trojan Horse it was named after . The Trojan Horse , at first glance will appear to be useful software but will actually do damage once installed or run on your computer . Those on the receiving end of a Trojan Horse are usually tricked into opening them because they appear to be receiving legitimate software or files from a legitimate source . When a Trojan is activated on your computer , the results can vary . Some Trojans are designed to be more annoying than malicious ( like changing your desktop , adding silly active desktop icons ) or they can cause serious damage by deleting files and destroying information on your system . Trojans are also known to create a backdoor on your computer that gives malicious users access to your system , possibly allowing confidential or personal information to be compromised . Unlike viruses and worms , Trojans do not reproduce by infecting other files nor do they self - replicate . URL_0

While there are lots of really plausible explanations for why the market moves a certain way on a certain day, no one really knows for sure.  In order to do that, you would need to understand the 'minds' of all the market players.  These days many of these players are secret proprietary algorithms.  I'm not quibbling with the specifics of these explanations (I have no better) just pointing out that these are just really hypotheses and if the market starts following different patterns, they will be tossed into the dust bin of 'old thinking'. I think the best thing you can explain to your son is that the stock market is basically a gigantic highly complex poker game.  The daily gyrations of the market are about individuals trying to predict where the herd is going to go next and then after that and then after that etc. If you want to help him understand the market, I suggest two things.  The first is to find or create a simple market game and play it with him.  The other would be to teach him about how bonds are priced and why prices move the way they do.  I know this might sound weird and most people think bonds are esoteric but there are bonds have a much simpler pricing model based on fundamental financial logic.  It's much easier then to get your head around the moves of the bond markets because the part of the price based on beliefs is much more limited (i.e. will the company be able pay & where are rates going.)  Once you have that understanding, you can start thinking about the different ways stocks can be valued (there are many) and what the market movements mean about how people are valuing different companies. With regard to this specific situation, here's a different take on it from the 'priced in' explanation which isn't really different but might make more sense to your son: Pretend for a second that at some point these stocks did move seasonally.  In the late fall and winter when sales went up, the stock price increased in kind.  So some smart people see this happening every year and realize that if they bought these stocks in the summer, they would get them cheap and then sell them off when they go up.  More and more people are doing this and making easy money.  So many people are doing it that the stock starts to rise in the Summer now.  People now see that if they want to get in before everyone else, they need to buy earlier in the Spring.  Now the prices start rising in the Spring.  People start buying in the beginning of the year... You can see where this is going, right?  Essentially, a strategy to take advantage of well known seasonal patterns is unstable.  You can't profit off of the seasonal changes unless everyone else in the market is too stupid to see that you are simply anticipating their moves and react accordingly.

Lots of political questions on here lately . Hard to answer these without being too biased . This is one of those things where you 'll have to sort through lots of information and figure out what you believe . I 'll state for the record that I am against US involvement in Syria , but I think I also have a pretty good understanding of the reasons for becoming involved . There is more than just a single reason the US wants to get involved in Syria , so I will try to break it down into pieces . Strategy ( geopolitics ): - The United States has been trying to keep the middle east under control for decades now . Part of this is because of its strategic alliance with Israel , part of this is because the US sees the middle east as the source of all of its problems with the ' war on terror ' and partly because of economic interests in the area ( oil mostly ) . - Syria is part of a much bigger ' game ' being played by the major world powers . As part of this struggle , each power is trying to gain powerful countries within each major ' region ' in order to try to influence that region 's politics and economics . Assad and the government of Syria have been allies with Russia for a long time , and represent a big part of Russia 's attempts to gain control of the middle east ( Iran potentially being the other ) . Russia has ALWAYS been trying to gain control of the middle east , or at least parts of it . This goes back to the 9th century and probably even earlier . This could be a major opportunity to deprive Russia of influence in the region and gain a US ally ( if things do n't go horribly wrong , as they have in the past ) . - The US wants to prevent Syria from becoming a ' failed state ' . If it looks as though Assad will lose , the US might support one of the ' better ' rebel factions to prevent ' worse ' rebel factions from winning the country and taking over . This is part of the US 's strategy in the global war on terror . -Economics -Many people will say the US is going into Syria for oil . This seems like a compelling argument but probably is n't true . Syria has had a great deal of oil in the past but seems to be running out . Its oil production has been decreasing lately and Syria is now an oil importing nation . If this is the real reason , the US government ca n't do the basic math it would take to show that the oil in Syria wo n't be worth the money . However , if you make the argument that controlling Syria is part of a bigger effort to control the entire middle east and the oil in the region , the argument might make more sense . Human Rights : - I honestly do n't think the US ' efforts in the Mid East are completely selfish . I think many in the US government see what is happening in Syria and want to help . There are many in the US government who still feel the US military can be used as a force for good in the world . Both the Syrian government and the rebels have been proven to have committed horrible human rights abuses . The citizens of Syria are genuinely suffering and the situation is becoming a major human rights crisis . I imagine plenty of people in the US government feel they are obligated to do something to help , and part of this needs to involve the US military . The UN seems to be looking for a way to stop the violence in Syria , but simply does not have the kind of power that would be necessary to do anything . Also , because Russia has a permanent seat the on UN security council , the UN will be completely powerless to interfere .

Repost : It 's hard to explain like you 're five , because being five is very much like being on LSD . But here goes : Stop and listen . Just listen . Hear those cars ? The hum of that fan ? The kids shouting on the next block ? The refrigerator 's motor ? Those are going on all the time , but you normally do n't notice them -- the mind filters them out because you ca n't pay attention to everything all the time . When you get older , the filter gets stronger . When you 're an adult , you can pass entire days without actually noticing much of anything except what you 're focusing on ( like work , or your mortgage payment ) . LSD , in small doses , dissolves that filter -- suddenly the world is full of sounds and smells and colors that are really always there but you do n't notice . A good example is " trails , " where moving things have an afterimage . People notice this on LSD , but really it happens all the time ( move your hand and you can see them if you 're looking for them , but normally you do n't because the mind filters them out so you can focus on more important things ( that is , pretty much anything ) . Now : Just because things are filtered out does n't mean they 're not real . LSD can help people see and understand things they normally wouldn't -- it can give you insights that last after the LSD trip is over . ( A trivial real - life example : on LSD I realized that a friend of mine looked like Mr. Potato Head and could never unsee it after I came down . A less trivial one : a friend of mine in college realized that everyone in his fraternity was an asshole and he needed to not hang out with them . ) LSD ( at low doses ) is n't always a positive experience ; sometimes you 're filtering out things you 'd rather not know ( like , your spouse really does n't like you anymore , or you hate your job ) . At higher doses , you 're not just seeing things you normally filter out ; you 're full - on hallucinating . Or so I 've heard ; I 'm not speaking from experience on that .
