Can confirm . Source : Am in the linen service industry . The majority of our customers pay by the pound of linen delivered . We have to closely monitor the weight of the linen because the linen loses weight after each wash . Even though it takes the same amount of chemicals and labor to process that piece of linen , it weighs less , so customers who pay by the pound get it at a lower price . This may seem insignificant on a piece by piece basis , but an average laundering facility serves a half - million pieces of linen per week . We expect a towel to lose 15%-25 % of its weight over its service life , which is , on average , six months . Occasionally we 'll find a towel that 's been in service for more than two years and you can practically see through it . We then remove that linen from service . An average commercial dryer can collect 25 - 50 pounds of lint per day . An average facility will have 10 - 15 dryers of that size . ( You can stand up inside of them . ) We actively control the lint collection process . Lint is incredibly flammable and any minor buildup is a state / federal safety violation . Similarly , I worked in a carpet mill that would fill up two dumpsters with lint per week . Recyclers would purchase the lint from us so they could reuse it in other industries . TLDR : All textiles lose lint during the wash process . Lint is serious business . Edit : Sincerest thanks for the Gold ! Commercial laundry is a behind - the - scenes industry that is appears everywhere . If I can answer any questions , let me know . It 's not quite AMA worthy .

First up , it 's not having an unusually hard time getting out of recession . These things take a lot of time to correct themselves . Nevertheless , Greece has some especially hard challenges , specifically Europe and Greece . First , Europe . The system should work like this : - when economic activity drops , a government automatically finds its tax base shrinking and its expenditures rising ( due to increased welfare , business assisstance etc . ) - this results in the government increasing its debt . ( Governments must always have debt to maintain liquidity in the credit market , but that 's another story ) - as economic activity drops and unemployment increases , so domestic production also drops . This causes imports to rise . - in turn this puts downward pressure on the value of the currency . Which acts to make imports more expensive . This is the start of the turnaround . - the lower exchange rate assists exporters to be more competitive with other countries . This means exports rise . - as exports rise domestic production goes up and unemployment goes down . - government expenditure drops and tax receipts go up . Also a decent amount of foreign ( high value ) money from exports finds its way to government . - this high value foreign money can be used to rapidly pay off government debt that was issued in the low value domestic money , and has dropped with the exchange rate . - as the debt is reduced , the exchange rate begins to rise and the economy starts to stabilise . However , in Europe the currency ( the Euro ) ca n't devalue unless the whole continent is in equally bad shape ( Which they are not ) . So the key event that triggers a recovery never occurs . As an alternative , Europe puts pressure on members who are in trouble to cut taxes and spending , in the hope that lower taxes increase the money supply , which is supposed to drive domestic demand and create recovery that way . But it often does n't work . That 's because a lot of spending in a downturn i d 's focused on keeping marginal players in the game earning money and paying tax . Cutting spending wipes these people out , reducing the tax base further and damaging the businesses they spend money at . This often means that the benefits of austerity are swallowed up I the extra damage it does . What would work is transferring money from parts of Europe that are doing well and spending it to support the marginal players in markets that are n't doing so well . This is largely how a country like the US stabilises state economies that ca n't trigger recovery by devaluing the currency . The second problem is Greece itself . As a former socialist state , the number of people avoiding tax but collecting welfare ( while working ) is very large , so whatever recovery might be possible , the culture of handouts and tax cheats makes it all that much harder

Well , stocks are just 1 part of " The Market " . It would take me days to explain to you different aspects of how the market works ; people get PhD 's in Finance and still ca n't fully comprehend certain aspects of how it moves . For the sake of conversation , let 's stick with stocks ( equity ) . You can go to yahoo finance and type in a 4 letter ticker symbol to find the stock price of any publicly traded company . The price of that stock is reflective of how the market feels about that company 's future . This price is based on earnings , expectations , current state of that industry , news etc . Even stupid things like the CEO getting caught cheating on his wife can cause a fluctuation in the value of a company 's stock . Just remember , something only has value if someone is willing to pay for it . The value of a stock is the value a buyer is willing to pay for it , and the value a owner is willing to sell it for . The fluctuation of the stock $ is a combination of attitudes of buyers & sellers . First thing I like to tell people about the stock market is the theory of Market Efficiency explained by Miller & Modigliani * which , in a nutshet , says that a stock 's current price reflects ALL information , both insider and public knowledge . If you think you can beat the market , you 're wrong , because once you know about the information , so does the rest of the world , and stock prices adjust immediately .

The first thing to understand is what the " M " means . The M means " modulation " . When a radio station broadcasts ( sends out , transmits , etc ) a signal , it uses what is called a " carrier wave " . You ca n't actually hear this signal , as it is a very high frequency ... way higher than your ears can detect . The actual music ( or talking ) is used at the radio station to change the constant nature of the carrier wave . This " change " is called modulation . It 's sort of " mixed in " . AM radio does this by altering the amplitude ( or volume or power ) of the carrier wave . FM does it by using it to change the frequency ( pitch ) of the carrier wave . Think of a rope tied to a wall . Grab one end of the rope and move your arm up and down . How high ( or low ) your arm gets is AM . Now move your arm up and down * the same distance * but change * how fast * you move your arm . That is FM . In radio , you can " put more information " in how fast you move your arm , compared to how high / low you can do it . ( This is called " bandwidth " ) In general , more sound information can be put in a typical FM signal , and so it tends to sound better . That 's also why you see stereo broadcasts ( left and right ear channels ) done almost always with FM .

I 'm also going to throw things into this answer ... First things first , the " * Shock " universe is a collection of different games with similar play - styles and stories but , there are not many connections between the games . Specifically , there are three different settings within the universe , and some common elements introduced by designer Ken Levine once he was on the project . * * System Shock / System Shock 2 * * These games were the start of it but , I 'm not too familiar with them . They 're old PC games from the 90s set in a futuristic cyber - punk universe . They were first person games with RPG elements so the player could build their character . In this setting , the player could augment his abilities with OS units and nanites while using melee and ranged weapons to fight against enemies . The player also gets a set of psionic powers that they can upgrade as well . These powers are more " magic " than " science " . Ken Levine first worked on System Shock 2 and he 's been the backer of every * Shock game since . * * Bioshock / Bioshock 2 * * These games were spiritual sequels to the System Shock line . Nothing was directly related but , gameplay and story elements were common between the two series . The setting was Rapture , a dystopia built underneath the Atlantic Ocean by Andrew Ryan in the 1950s . Ryan believed that true freedom meant that people should own their own work , and that they should n't be stopped from doing something because of another moral agenda . Artists should n't be censored , researchers should n't have to muck about in red - tape to advance their fields through experimentation on humans or animals , etc . One of the inventions of Rapture were plasmids , which granted magical powers , and were quite addicting . The player comes in after Rapture has fallen apart and a bunch of junkies have taken over . Bioshock 2 picks up years later , after the first game 's protagonist has left , and they play through another aftermath of Rapture 's demise . * * Bioshock Infinite * * This is a spiritual successor as well to the System Shock and Bioshock games . The title is a little misleading , and there may be more story elements of Infinite tied into what is to become Bioshock but , for the most part , the setting is completely separate . Infinite takes place in Columbia , a floating All American City , in the 1920s . There are fun magical augments like the predecessor games and the general story elements are going to be the same as the others . You can play Bioshock Infinite without ever having played any other games in the * Shock series . There may be some references to the past titles but , nothing story - wise is going to be tied together to the other games . I would highly suggest playing through all of them though if given the chance ( or at least the Bioshocks ... I ca n't really comment on the System Shock games because I 've only read about them ) Hope this helps and does n't give too much away . The main thing I 'm trying to get at , I guess , is that Infinite ( to my knowledge ) is n't related to any of the other games in the series via plot elements . Nothing you know or do n't know about Bioshock 1/2 is going to have an impact on the plot of Infinite ( although being familiar with the gameplay elements may be useful )

This has been asked a few times ( [ here ] ( URL_3 ) and [ here ] ( URL_0 ) ) , here 's what I posted last time : It depends , there are all kinds of viruses . A lot of the most new and innovative viruses are proof - of - concept viruses . These are generally made by skilled * hackers * ( and I do mean [ hackers ] ( URL_2 ) here , not crackers et al ) , who , in the process of learning about computer systems have discovered a vulnerability and want to see if it can be exploited , not to actually do anything malicious , just to see how it would be done , to satisfy their curiosity . The [ morris worm ] ( URL_1 ) , one of the first recorded internet viruses , is one such virus . These viruses tend to have no payload , and thus do no harm ( the morris worm did harm only accidentally ) . Other viruses do harm , but only mischievous or annoying harm . These tend to be created by script kiddies who know enough to be able to write them , but not enough to know that they should n't . Often these viruses will announce their presence loudly and gloat , generally with the intention that the script kiddie gains notoriety as a leet haxor , but this of course does n't happen . The last , worst and most recent type of virus is the truly malicious virus . Since people started doing real business online , banking online , and storing personal information on their machines , there has been a growing financial incentive to crack peoples computer security systems ( such as they are ) . These viruses will tend to make an effort to hide their presence and remain undetected , while they send off personal data for use in identity theft , log keystrokes to steal passwords used online , pop up ads for profit , etc . They tend to be written and managed by organised crime syndicates and other unpleasant characters who only became interested in computers when the opportunity for illegal profit became apparent . A note on the hacker / cracker thing ; There are a lot of people , who may write the viruses i mentioned first , who are involved in all of this out of curiosity , obsession and interest in understanding the technology world , and these people get annoyed at those who are arrogant and disrespectful , or those who misuse their skill for profit to the detriment of others . It 's as though hackers are devoted to a martial art , learning self control , discipline and dedication , while script kiddies are 14 year old kids who have seen too many kung - fu movies and want to just learn some easy flashy moves to show off , and criminal crackers are assholes who use their fighting skill on innocent civilians to take their wallets . So ' people who make viruses ' can be very different from one another , making different types of viruses for very different reasons . Do n't group them together .

Like most thing in real life , it 's complicated . Private industry are really good at efficiency . If there is a way to keep the production cost low they will find it . And if you have competition that will keep the selling cost low . The more regulation you put on a company , the higher the production cost become . They need to pay for security , increase the worker salary , pay more administration to make sure they follow all regulation , etc . If the production cost increase , they will increase the selling cost or will do less profit . Less profit mean that less investor will want to put their money into the company , which mean that the company will growth less and will created less jobs . Additionally , when you add more regulation it usually cost more to the state because they need people to prepare or make sure that everybody follow these regulation . If it cost more to the state , it need to increase taxes , which also slow down the growth of the economy . But of course , private ownership only have one goal of more profit . So regulation are needed to protect the rights of worker , environment , safety , etc . So the goal is to have a good balance because too much regulation mean less growth of the economy and not enough mean problems for the community . What is that balanced ideal position is a hot topic for debate . Not everybody agree on how much is too much and how much is n't enough .

In terms of biology , the parts of our brain that are responsible for decision making are the newest , and often do not finish development until our 20s . The latter is fairly self explanatory , unfinished development results in subpar results . The other part is a bit trickier . Think of how most people react to bugs . In the modern world , finding a spider in your bathroom creeps people out and scares them much more that it rationally should . But evolution is a tinkerer , not an engineer . It was prudent in the past to have a heightened fear of bugs for fear of diseases and such . We still have those parts of our brain though , and they are older and thus come to the forefront first . It is why seeing that spider makes us want to smash it with a shoe , when in reality it poses no threat to us . In the past it was beneficial to make quick and bold decisions , such as fight or flight . Now if you are thinking something through and still going to make a bad choice . It is because of a wide array of methods we use to rationalize it . We essentially bullshit ourselves to tell us what we want to hear . In addition , we often overestimate our capabilities and have poor foresight . Any economist or psychologist will tell you that humans have terrible long term decision making skills .

They do n't know you are n't a thief . However some locks and some keys are protected from this with security measures . Locksmiths are n't worried about copying a house key . But if you try to get a key for a high security lock copied , it 's not going to happen . These keys will often have writing on them for " do not copy " and use multiple rows of pins . The only time that a locksmith might want to verify your identity is if you are asking them to get into a locked car , house , or business . They need reasonable assurances that you are authorized to be there and to enter the premises . If it turns out that you are lying and the police get involved the locksmith has an out if they took reasonable precautions to ensure you were authorized . Source - I 'm an amateur lock smith with about 10 years experience keying , re - pinning , picking , repairing , and bypassing locks . Locks do not keep someone from breaking in to a home or vehicle in any case . They are there to keep honest people honest , and to deter thieves to pick easier targets . If someone wants to steal from you , there is not a lot you can do to stop them short of guarding your property 24x7 . However you can take reasonable precautions so you are n't the low hanging fruit when a thief wants to break in . And if someone does want to steal , they are not going to use a locksmith who could be a witness against them . They will simply smash and grab , or con their way on premises . And being a locksmith , if I wanted to break the law and make a key I do n't need the original key to copy . I can cut my own key for most locks using a few simple tricks for pin lengths . But I would n't bother . Most residential locks can be opened in under 10 seconds by an amateur simply by raking .

That 's a great question that took surprisingly long into the history of physics to figure out ! I 'll skip the historical details ( [ although you should read about it here ] ( URL_0 ) ) and cut to the chase : It turns out that everything with a non - zero temperature emits light all the time . This is n't necessarily the type of light that your eyes can detect , but it 's there all the same . It was discovered experimentally long before it was explained theoretically ( it took until the year 1900 ) , but now we have a pretty good understanding of what 's happening inside things when they glow . In fact , it 's the same reason that a hot object in a fire will begin to glow red , and why fire itself is orange at the top . This radiation is called Blackbody Radiation , named so because the form of the radiation is derived by assuming that the body is completely black , i.e. , it absorbs all incoming light and does n't scatter or reflect any . The only light that is emitted is actually generated inside the object , not just reflected from a nearby flashlight . The useful thing about blackbody radiation is that the spectrum of light that 's emitted is determined by the temperature and nothing else . That means that we can measure the spectrum of emitted blackbody radiation from an object and immediately know its temperature . There are some caveats though ; some materials do n't play by the rules , instead choosing to emit light that 's not well - described by the blackbody radiation spectrum . Luckily for us , the sun is one of the most ideal blackbody radiators that we 've discovered : that means our estimate of the temperature ( on the surface anyway ) is really quite accurate .

Generally , the answer is : " because friction . " Almost any machine has friction in it somewhere . Even if it 's hidden deep inside , in the bearings that allow the shafts to rotate smoothly . Every time the machine moves , some of the energy of that rotation is scraped away ( transformed into heat ) by friction . Another form of friction that perpetual motion machine inventors often forget about is air drag . If the machine moves at all , it will be slowed down by air drag , and eventually stop . In theory , it 's possible to have perpetual motion in a universe without friction . But our universe does have friction , so ... In order to get around this problem , perpetual motion whackos often resort to " machines " that incorporate electricity or magnetism . They claim that the electrical or magnetic parts of the machine compensate for the mechanical friction . Unfortunately this still does n't work . All electrical components in the real world have some amount ( however small ) or electrical resistance . This electrical resistance is the electrical equivalent of friction . It inevitably burns up some amount of your electricity as heat . It 's usually a lot smaller than mechanical friction , but it 's still there and it WILL eventually stop any electrical " perpetual motion " machine . See URL_0 and especially see the highly entertaining [ Museum Of Unworkable Devices . ] ( URL_1 )

I 'm not sure this is it , so do n't quote this as fact , but I would say that it 's because of the fight or flight response . If you 've ever eaten way too much and then exercised really hard , you 've probably notice that you tend to throw up . This is the body 's last resort when you need fast energy ; chemical energy takes a long time to get into body , and your cells store enough to function all the time . Burning energy , on the other hand , is a very fast process - but one that requires as much oxygen as possible . To get more oxygen , you need to pump your blood faster . Unfortunately , digestion requires your blood to be moving slowly ; this is why we become sedentary when we have a lot to digest . So , when you have something in your stomach that 's being digested , but you need all the energy you can possibly get , the body 's response is to vomit up whatever is in your stomach , so that you can get as much oxygen to your muscles as possible . When you 're nervous about asking a girl out or something like that , it may ( and this is where I 'm forming a hypothesis based on the evidence ) be triggering a fight or flight response - the butterflies in your stomach are your body 's way of getting ready in case it needs to take emergency action .

These people are literally all wrong . The real answer depends what level you 're referring to . If you 're talking a global scale , it 's because of the increase in technology and the decrease of inefficiency in the market . Tech increase means any advancement that increases real output , such as a better way to farm or a way to make better houses . Decrease in market inefficiency are things such as removing minimum wages , tariffs , etc . Note , these things may lead to a worse economy in one country , but the global economy as a whole will constantly grow . People saying bullshit about capitalism and unsustainable growth are completely misinterpreting Marx . If you 're referring to an individual country level , then growth occurs due to the things mentioned above , but also at the expense of other countries . This much is self explanatory . The reason the economy shrinking is bad is because technology is always increasing such that real output of the entire global system is always increasing . If one country is sitting there and staying flat or shrinking , that means it is losing relative economic strength compared to other countries . This tends to have a compounding effect as the country is forced to do things that cause the economy to become even weaker . An example is minimum wage . If the US was n't hemorrhaging high skilled labor ( such as techs at a chip fabrication plant ) then the US may not need to have to increase its minimum wage to bring the lowest standard deviation of the population to livable wages . But alas the US is shitting jobs like mad and Walmart workers get paid scraps so the US has to introduce heavy inefficiency to the system by raising the minimum wage . Edit : Please note I 'm being 100 % serious about everyone else saying a load of bullshit . Money does n't inflate for fun . It can deflate just as easily if you feel like making it that way , a la bitcoin . Saying money magically decreases in value just for the sake of it is not real economics . There has to be some kind of underlying reason , as money is not some kind of natural force of the physical world .

It 's all about range and the ability to attack at range . In WWII , when battleships were still being used in significant numbers , the upper limit of your ability to attack the enemy was limited by your ability to visually see the target . That visual limit was the curvature of the earth ( since oceans are relative ' flat ' surface ) . A battleship could only reliably attack what it could visually see . And given that boats were big , relatively slow moving things , you wanted to know where your enemies were so you could begin moving towards them . Recon planes aided battleships to an extent , but it did not drastically increase their ability to hit targets at extreme ranges . Even the advent of radar did not help battleships because the curvature of the earth still inhibits their ability to see . Enter the aircraft carrier - a ship capable of launching attack planes that could not only go beyond the curvature of the planet , but attack targets as well . All of a sudden , you have a ship capable of spotting and destroying targets beyond visual range in a reliable fashion . This is a huge advantage in naval warfare because , unlike in ground combat , you have no cover - you 're best means of defense is keeping as much distance between you and your opponent as possible . The ability to attack targets that are incapable of retaliating against your ships is a massive advantage . And since boats are , again , slow moving things , a battleship has no real means to engage an aircraft carrier . It can not ' surprise ' one because it will be seen coming from miles away by the planes . If a battleship could suddenly plop itself within gun range of an aircraft carrier , it would be a no - contest for the battleship to win . But that will never be the case , and thus the battleship fell into obsolescence . Cost of building / maintaining only entered into the equation in the sense that you do n't spend money on things that are n't useful ; and battleships simply were n't useful anymore in naval warfare . Ground combat , however , is a much different story than naval warfare . You might have guns that let you shoot as far as your eyes can see , but there might be hills , mountains , and buildings in the way . The terrain obstructs your ability to see enemies , and thus your ability to keep them at a distance . As a result , you can not necessarily rely on weapons that have limited defenses in close - ranges because you have less effective control over your ability to keep distance . But when those distances can be maintained , you do see extensive use of these long - range tools - artillery cannons and airstrips allow one to , again , engage at long range with limited enemy ability to retaliate . And air support is becoming an increasingly larger facet of ground wars . But dense terrain , particularly urban environments , necessitate the need for an armoured vehicle that can exert great power and survive retaliation from enemies at close quarters . You can bombard a city to rubble , but in the end you 'll still need to send in the army to clear the city out , and they 'll need the armour support to maintain a tactical advantage over enemies .

Marx talked about alienation , anyone who thinks about history / society in terms of alienation would be looking at history through a Marxist lens . The creation of subreddits has divided people up and created communities based on their interests in different subjects ( cats , science , jokes , trump , etc ) rather than where they live . People are now alienated from their geographic communities similar to the way they were alienated from the value of their labor according to Marx . lots of text incoming ... For Marx the primary issue with Capitalism is that the worker is alienated from the value of his labor . In olden days if a man built a chair he then had a chair ... he could keep it , sell it to his neighbor , whatever . In modern capitalist society workers in chair factories do nt keep the chairs they make , they do nt own the chair , its the companies chair , they do nt decide how much to sell the chair for or who to sell it to ... they just work there at the chair factory . They are alienated from the chair , the product of their labor . This is the only way capitalism can work , if they were nt alienated from the chair they would know the true value of the chair and their labor that went into the chair , then they would only agree to build the chair if the factory owner offered them a fair deal ( they are paid the exact value of 1 chair to produce 1 chair ) . The factory owner could never sell the chairs and make a profit ! And if he did make a profit on a chair the worker would then demand that new higher chair price for their labor the next time they build a chair . The capitalist must exploit the worker to make a profit and be successful ( I will give you 90 % of the value of 1 chair to make 1 chair ) . The benefits of this system should be obvious , rather than making a chair only when he needs one or his neighbor wants to buy one , the worker can build chairs all day and perfect his methods . The capitalist can focus on finding people to buy the chair . These are good things , the business expands and thrives , this is economic growth ! However , this is also a slippery slope ( I 'll give you 89 % of the value of 1 chair ... now I 'll give you 88 % ... 87 % ... etc ) and eventually this growth - driving and mutually beneficial exploitation of the worker becomes a 1 sided relationship ( I will give you 1 % of the value of the chair or I will find someone else who is poorer than you that will ) . These are the conditions Marx observed in factories in England , poor working class people living in awful conditions while factory owners thrived . His solution was simple , everyone in the factory splits an equal share of the profits , extend this to all factories and society at large ... this is Communism .

Certain operations really need to be allowed to finish without interruption . One example that comes to mind is hard drive access . Different systems do this in different ways , so I 'll make up a simple example . Suppose when you save a new file you write it to the disk like this : 1 . Find the next bit of empty space 2 . Write a header that says " This file is called ' myfile.txt ' and is 10 MB long " 3 . Write the file data And to read a file you do this : 1 . Find the header with the right name 2 . Read how long the file is from the header 3 . Read out that much data from the disk As I said , stupidly simple , no system actually works that way , it 's an example . But suppose that you 're writing that file and 5 MB of the way through you power the machine off . Now you have a header that says it 's for a 10 MB file with only 5 MB of actual file after it . Perhaps when you come to write the next file , " myotherfile.txt " , it looks for the next empty space and finds it after the 5 MB of data you wrote , so that 's where it puts the next file . Now if you try to read myfile.txt , you get the first 5 MB of the file , followed by the header for myotherfile.txt , and then the first 5 MB or so of myotherfile.txt , which is completely not what you thought was there . And if you try to make a change to the second half of myfile.txt , you completely clobber myotherfile.txt , since you 've overwritten its header . Real file - systems are more complicated , but that 's the kind of problem you get , when the machine is halfway through doing something which ca n't be left half - done . Edit : One common thing in computing is the problem you get when more than one system wants to use the same resource . Like you 're on skype and you 're browsing the web , and both programs want to use the network card to send data , but they ca n't both use it at the same time . One way to solve that problem is with locks . A program claims the resource and ' locks ' it , uses it for what it needs to do , then releases the lock . Other programs that want to use the resource at the same time try to get the lock and find they ca n't , so they know they have to wait . Problem is , what happens if something claims the lock , and then the system is powered off while it 's working ? It never gets a chance to release the lock , so the resource stays locked and nothing can use it . Again no network system actually works like that , but lock - file problems are something that can happen if you power off a machine while it 's working .

You have 3 doors , A , B and C. One contains a cash prize and the other two contain dud prizes . Monty Hall ( the game show host ) happens to know what each door contains . Let 's say you select A. Monty Hall opens door B and shows a dud prize , then offers you the opportunity to trade in your door for the remaining closed one . Should you make this switch ? You should switch , your chance of being correct goes from 33 % to 67 % . I too struggled with understanding it at first ( as I 'm sure every person ever did ) but there is two ways of thinking about it that really helped me . Before we get to that though , make sure you understand that Monty Hall knowing which door contains the cash is important . My first way of thinking about it . Think of it in the situation shown above , you 've selected A. There 's 3 outcomes to this situation . * Prize is behind A , Monty Hall has opened door B ( or C ) and offered you the chance to trade . You trade , you lose . * Prize is behind B , Monty Hall has opened door C and offered you the opportunity to trade . You trade , you win . * Prize is behind C , Monty Hall has opened door B and offered you the opportunity to trade . You trade , you win . From this you can see that 2/3 times you win by trading . My second way . Imagine the same scenario , but with 100 doors . If you selected door 1 , Monty Hall knows where the prize is hidden and decides to open 98 dud doors for you , would you trade ? How confident are you that your initial door was correct ? The odds of it being in your initial door are still 1/100 , but the odds of you being wrong are 99/100 .

An internal combustion ( gas , diesel , etc . ) engine is an air pump at heart , you are just adding a little fuel and spark to the mix to get power out of it . Most engines in use today are 4 cycle ( intake , compression , power , exhaust ) . * A mixture of air and fuel is pulled into the combustion chamber by the piston moving downward in the cylinder . * The piston compresses the mix by moving upward in the cylinder . * The mix is ignited , either by a spark plug or the compression alone is sufficient to cause ignition . * The ignited mix expands rapidly , pushing the piston back down the cylinder . * The piston moves back up the cylinder pushing the exhaust gases out , completing the 4th cycle . During the power cycle , the flame front is moving fast enough to be considered an explosion and it creates a shockwave that ends up traveling with the exhaust gases . This shockwave is the sound you end up hearing coming from the exhaust . The reason high performance , racing , engines are louder than vehicles you see on the road has a lot to do with regulations and the general desire of everyone not to be deafened . A lot of it comes from regulation on noise levels , some from pollution regulations , the biggest reason is performance for the race engines . Increasing the performance of an engine is all about increasing its volumetric efficiency , getting more air in so more fuel can be added to that air to make a hotter fire to push the piston harder . There are many ways to make this happen but the easiest way is to remove restrictions on the intake and exhaust sides of the air flow . Most race engines only have enough exhaust pipes to keep the fumes and flames from hitting the driver . Regulations on noise and pollution are not a concern , maximum performance and safety are the goal . For road vehicles , meeting those regulations are the goal . The exhaust system must take the gases to a place where they can be expelled without filling the passenger cabin . It has to provide for pollution regulation with a catalytic converter , these are generally not as restrictive as people think since the pellet type was replaced with honeycomb catalyst material . They also provide for noise regulation with a muffler that dampens the sound . The way a muffler is built gases are given a pretty straight path but sound waves are reflected around quite a bit making them weaker and quieter . You can very easily see this effect for yourself . Just get a sock , preferably clean , and put it in your mouth . Now take a deep breath and yell . Remove the sock and repeat .

Apparently the part of the human brain that 's stimulated by melody is left untouched by just about every other animal . According to brain imaging , your dog has no sense of rhythm . Your cat certainly does n't " get " that CD you 're listening to , and your hamster ca n't even remember and recognise a simple melody played over and over . Perhaps it 's because animals live more " in the moment " , so the fact that we introduce time from our observation is what enables us to take the isolated notes and see them as a bigger picture - but that 's taking us into quantum physics and not really explaining it like you 're five . If you 're really interested , think about an arrow in flight . If you were to freeze time , you would know where the arrow was , but you could n't say how fast it was travelling , or where it would land . In any given moment , the arrow is frozen in its point . You can search for more on this if you 're bothered . But my suggestion would be that dogs ( for example ) only hear the notes that are being played " now " , rather than making sense of the whole musical bar or getting the underlying rhythm from 16 repeated sounds . I agree that some birds can mimic other sounds , but they are just as likely to mimic the sound of a car starting or aircon fans powering up as they are to mimic a tune . TLDR - No .

During a fight , do you find yourself saying " BUT I NEVER SAID THAT ! " , or perhaps " YOU'RE INTENTIONALLY TWISTING MY MEANING ! " You 're the victim of deception . Your opponent is using " straw man fallacy " against you . Since they can not defeat you honestly , they first must distort your argument , and then attack the warped version . . But sometimes it 's not intentionally dishonest . Angry people tend to mis - hear your arguments . You can state your case in different ways over and over , and every time they 'll misinterpret , they 'll only hear a warped version . Communication is failing , and it causes them to attack a " Straw Man " because they 're responding to something that you never said or meant . This problem is even worse if hatred is involved : if they consider themselves superior and you 're supposedly stupid . In that case they simply can not believe the complicated concepts or even obvious truth of what you 're saying . Instead they 'll subconsciously warp it in a way that makes you look ignorant or even evil . Then they 'll fight against the warped version they 've created . This " White Knight " thinks they 're fighting ignorance and defeating an evil foe , when actually they 're deceiving themselves by attacking a " straw man " which was produced by their own hot emotions . This sort of unintended straw man argument is often the core of many troll battles / flamewars . And it shows why honest straightforward discussions are often impossible if heated emotions are involved .

I am not aware of a version of Interac available in the U.S., but there are alternative ways to receive money: Cheque. The problem with mailed cheques is that they take time to deliver, and time to clear. If you ship your wares before the cheque has cleared and the cheque is bad, you're out the merchandise. COD. How this works is you place a COD charge on your item at the post office in the amount you charge the customer. The post office delivers the package on the other end when the customer pays. The post office pays you at the time you send the package. There is a fee for this, talk to your local post office or visit the Canada Post website. Money order. Have your U.S. customers send an International Money Order, not a Domestic Money Order. Domestic money orders can only be cashed at a U.S. post office. The problem here is again delivery time, and verifying your customer sent an International Money Order. It can be a pain to have to send back a Domestic Money Order to a customer explaining what they have to do to pay you, even more painful if you don't catch the error before shipping your wares. Credit Card. There are a number of companies offering credit card processing that are much cheaper than a bank. PayPal, Square, and Intuit are three such companies offering these services. After I did my investigations I found Square to be the best deal for me. Please do your own research on these companies (and banks!) and find out which one makes the most sense for you. Some transaction companies may forbid the processing of payment for e-cig materials as they my be classed as tobacco.

There is change , we have elections every two years ( four for president ) Every two years we vote on the entire house of representatives ( half of congress , the legislative - lawmaking branch of government ) , and 1/3 of the senate ( the other half of congress ) . In general , people keep electing the same people as long as they run again . There are two main reasons for it . 1 . Redistricting - Every 10 years when a new census comes out lines have to be redrawn to accurately decide how much representation in congress each state gets . This is often used as an opportunity for the party that is in charge in each state to change the lines so that it favors them . Over time this means that * * most * * districts lean heavily one way or another . This means that if you 're a republican or democrat in the house of representatives , if you run again next term , the guy from the other party probably wo n't be able to beat you . There are laws to limit this effect , although it still happens . Redistricting is n't a factor in the senate . 2 . People like * * their representative * * even if they do n't like congress . While people say they do n't like congress , they still like the guy they voted for . This is partly because of # 1 above but it 's a common situation . This is because even though congress makes decisions a lot of people dislike , their representative will often vote how they prefer . The reason congress does n't agree is mostly because they 'll be replaced next election if they work with the other side . * * So most people are upset with congress , but when they have a chance to change a small part of it , they overwhelmingly will choose to not do so . * * A small majority are upset with the president , but he ca n't run again in 2016 anyway . The US may replace him with a republican ( switching is common after two terms of the other party ) or they might replace him with a different democrat . Data and information visualization (data viz or info viz) is an interdisciplinary field that deals with the graphic representation of data and information. It is a particularly efficient way of communicating when the data or information is numerous as for example a time series.
It is also the study of visual representations of abstract data to reinforce human cognition. The abstract data include both numerical and non-numerical data, such as text and geographic information. It is related to infographics and scientific visualization. One distinction is that it's information visualization when the spatial representation (e.g., the page layout of a graphic design) is chosen, whereas it's scientific visualization when the spatial representation is given.
From an academic point of view, this representation can be considered as a mapping between the original data (usually numerical) and graphic elements (for example, lines or points in a chart). The mapping determines how the attributes of these elements vary according to the data. In this light, a bar chart is a mapping of the length of a bar to a magnitude of a variable. Since the graphic design of the mapping can adversely affect the readability of a chart, mapping is a core competency of Data visualization.
Data and information visualization has its roots in the field of statistics and is therefore generally considered a branch of descriptive statistics. However, because both design skills and statistical and computing skills are required to visualize effectively, it is argued by authors such as Gershon and Page that it is both an art and a science.
Research into how people read and misread various types of visualizations is helping to determine what types and features of visualizations are most understandable and effective in conveying information.

Banks are essentially big pools of money . The day to day banking you use personally ( checking , savings , debit cards , etc ) is not the reason they exist . That 's an extra service they do to make just a little more money than they would otherwise . [ Big banking ] ( URL_1 ) is divided into 3 types : 1 . A [ Central Bank ] ( URL_2 ) " circulates money on behalf of a government and acts as its monetary authority by implementing monetary policy , which regulates the money supply " . That 's the government bank that prints the money . 2 . A [ Commercial Bank ] ( URL_4 ) " accepts deposits and pools those funds to provide credit , either directly by lending , or indirectly by investing through the capital markets . Within the global financial markets , these institutions connect market participants with capital deficits ( borrowers ) to market participants with capital surpluses ( investors and lenders ) by transferring funds from those parties who have surplus funds to invest ( financial assets ) to those parties who borrow funds to invest in real assets " . Later , I 'll assume this is the one you 're asking about . 3 . A [ savings bank ] ( URL_3 ) " is similar to a savings and loan association ( S & L ) . They can either be stockholder owned or mutually owned , in which case they are permitted to only borrow from members of the financial cooperative . The asset structure of savings banks and savings and loan associations is similar , with residential mortgage loans providing the principal assets of the institution 's portfolio " . A bank that loans money to other banks ( usually commercial banks ) . Basically , it 's not one bank that 's doing everything in the economy , different types all work together . What they have in common is that money goes out , just to different people , and how / why money goes in . The primary business of banking is not the small transactions like you using a debit card to pay for groceries or depositing a paycheck . The big money is in loans , whether they 're as small as one to buy a used car or as large as the [ $ 9 trillion the Federal Reserve made to US banks ] ( URL_0 ) . Smaller loans tend to be packaged up into blocks of debt and traded around between banks . Say Bank 1 ( a commercial bank ) borrowed X money from Bank 2 ( a savings bank ) at Y% interest . To pay it back , Bank 1 loans out enough money to cover that entire debt , then paid it to Bank 2 by literally giving them a package of debt . The people who borrowed from Bank 1 do n't know it , but all their payments essentially get funneled to Bank 2 . Bank 1 and Bank 2 are essentially betting on how much of the debt will be repaid or end up being worth more than expected . This is also why credit ratings are so important . If you have a bad credit rating ( as a person ) , your debt ca n't be packaged up and used to pay a savings bank so easily . if you 're a country , bad credit means international banks or other country 's banks do n't want to loan to you because you probably wo n't pay it back . So why is it necessary ? Debt is now how everything works . If you ca n't borrow , it 's harder to make big purchases , so things that are expensive stop selling except to the few rich people / groups there are . Big banks make even the most absurdly expensive loans possible ( like the previously mentioned $ 9 trillion in loans ) , so as long as people pay back their debts , it all continues to work .

A few thoughts ... Water is conductive , so even if the device is powered off , where water bridges the contacts of the battery and connects it to other places on the circuit , you can now be potentially dumping a whole batteries worth of power into small components that can not handle it . Even without a battery , it also does n't mean the circuit is safe however , as components like capacitors can hold an electrical charge for significant lengths of time , and again if that current gets to the wrong point there can be serious damage . Water can also damage some components by just being there - components that rely on precision spacing , movement or contact ( thing the layers of a touch screen maybe , or the moving parts of an accelerometer ) can be damaged if a physical item breaches this . Water is also corrosive to metals , and due to the tiny sizes of components and circuit board traces used in consumer electronics , this can take hold quickly and destructively . A protected ( painted ) steel beam may take decades to rust to the point of worry , but a tiny trace a fraction of a millimeter thick wo n't last long . Water is also rarely pure , but comes with a wide and varied collection of other materials dissolved in it - an obvious one being saltwater , which is hugely corrosive ( far more than pure water ) and will dry out leaving deposits of salt behind , which will continue to corrode and damage the circuitry .

As far as I know ( there may be some rare exceptions ) all neurons fire most powerfully the first time they 've fired in the last few minutes . They take some time to " recharge . " When you activate a neuron or set of neurons repeatedly , it diminishes the effect of the neuron activation . Think of flushing a toilet before the tank finishes filling . Does n't work as well the second time , does it ? Eventually , the neural cascade of " oh , those neurons fired and that means I 'm smelling xyz " does n't work anymore because the signal is too weak . You can demonstrate this with a phenomenon called " semantic satiation . " Repeat a word one or two dozen times in a row without stopping . Vegetable . Vegetable . Vegetable . Again and again . Eventually , the word will be gibberish to you . You know it exists , but you feel like it might be spelled wrong , or you ca nt quite remember what it means . You know you know , but you just ca n't remember . It 's on the tip of your tongue . Is a potato a fruit or a vegetable ? Who knows ? Does vegetable have three E 's or four ? All the neurons directly and indirectly related to the satiated word have been desensitized . The solution to desensitized neurons is to stop them from firing and allow them to recover . Get away from the smell for awhile . Go outside for a few minutes ( maybe 30 or so ) and when you come back insides you 'll notice the leftover food in the sink you forgot to wash away or the chicken wrapper in the trash that 's attracting flies .

Bits are like lanes of a road . A 32 bit system has 32 lanes , 64 bits have twice as many . Older computers were 16 and 8 bits . So the information is like traffic . More lanes lets more traffic through an area ... its faster . 32 bit programs do nt know the computer has 64 bits available , so they ignore those . Then again , a 32 bit program wo nt run on 16 bit system , nor will 64 bit applications run on 32 bit computers . They do nt know how to operate on less . They are nt designed to . The bit count also limits the biggest number that the computer can count , and this has influence on how much memory it can access . Each portion of memory has an address , not unlike a house number . A 32 bit computer can handle 2 to the power of 32 addresses . That s 4,294,967,295 spots of memory or about 4 gigs . A 64 bit machine can address much much more : 18,446,744,073,709,551,615 bits of ram , or about 4 billion times more ! The difference between two programs is mostly invisible to the user , but behind the scenes , its quite dramatic . If we had an 8 bit computer , it would store the number one like this : 0000 0001 meaning 8 places of zero or one . 16 bits would be twice as big : 0000 0000 0000 0001 32 bits are 2 times larger than 16 bit , and 64 bits is 2 times larger yet . So each generation of computer program is twice as large as the last , if it only does the same things . If you are thinking the new MS word app does nt do much more than the old one , you are right . Programs are getting less efficient(in some ways ) at using resources available to them . They have all that extra number space , and human activities do nt normally deal with such large things . So it seems likely that 128 bit computers will not ever be needed , though its likely that manufacturers will make them anyway , just to have something new to sell .

The limit on SEP IRA is 25%, not 20%.  If you're self-employed (filing on Schedule C), then it's taken on net earning, which in your example would be 25% of $90,000. (https://www.irs.gov/retirement-plans/retirement-plans-for-self-employed-people) JoeTaxpayer is correct as regards the 401(k) limits.  The elective deferrals are per person - That's a cap in sum across multiple plans and across both traditional and Roth if you have those.  In general, it's actually across other retirement plan types too - See below. If you're self-employed and set-up a 401(k) for your own business, the elective deferral is still aggregated with any other 401(k) plans in which you participate that year, but you can still make the employer contribution on your own plan. This IRS page is current a pretty good one on this topic:  https://www.irs.gov/retirement-plans/one-participant-401k-plans Key quotes that are relevant: The business owner wears two hats in a 401(k) plan: employee and employer. Contributions can be made to the plan in both capacities. The owner can contribute both: •Elective deferrals up to 100% of compensation (“earned income” in the case of a self-employed individual) up to the annual contribution limit:  ◦$18,000 in 2015 and 2016, or $24,000 in 2015 and 2016 if age 50 or over; plus •Employer nonelective contributions up to:  ◦25% of compensation as defined by the plan, or ◦for self-employed individuals, see discussion below It continues with this example: The amount you can defer (including pre-tax and Roth contributions) to all your plans (not including 457(b) plans) is $18,000 in 2015 and 2016. Although a plan's terms may place lower limits on contributions, the total amount allowed under the tax law doesn’t depend on how many plans you belong to or who sponsors those plans. EXAMPLE Ben, age 51, earned $50,000 in W-2 wages from his S Corporation in 2015. He deferred $18,000 in regular elective deferrals plus $6,000 in catch-up contributions to the 401(k) plan. His business contributed 25% of his compensation to the plan, $12,500. Total contributions to the plan for 2015 were $36,500. This is the maximum that can be contributed to the plan for Ben for 2015. A business owner who is also employed by a second company and participating in its 401(k) plan should bear in mind that his limits on elective deferrals are by person, not by plan. He must consider the limit for all elective deferrals he makes during a year. Notice in the example that Ben contributed more that than his elective limit in total (his was $24,000 in the example because he was old enough for the $6,000 catch-up in addition to the $18,000 that applies to everyone else).  He did this by declaring an employer contribution of $12,500, which was limited by his compensation but not by any of his elective contributions. Beyond the 401(k), keep in mind that elective contributions are capped across different types of retirement plans as well, so if you have a SEP IRA and a solo 401(k), your total contributions across those plans are also capped.  That's also mentioned in the example. Now to the extent that you're considering different types of plans, that's a whole question in itself - One that might be worth consulting a dedicated tax advisor.  A few things to consider (not extensive list): As for payroll / self-employment tax: Looks like you will end up paying Medicare, including the new "Additional Medicare" tax that came with the ACA, but not SS: If you have wages, as well as self-employment   earnings, the tax on your wages is paid first.   But this rule only applies if your total earnings   are more than $118,500. For example, if you   will have $30,000 in wages and $40,000 in selfemployment   income in 2016, you will pay the   appropriate Social Security taxes on both your   wages and business earnings. In 2016, however,   if your wages are $78,000, and you have $40,700   in net earnings from a business, you don’t pay   dual Social Security taxes on earnings more   than $118,500. Your employer will withhold   7.65 percent in Social Security and Medicare   taxes on your $78,000 in earnings. You must   pay 15.3 percent in Social Security and Medicare   taxes on your first $40,500 in self-employment   earnings and 2.9 percent in Medicare tax on the   remaining $200 in net earnings. https://www.ssa.gov/pubs/EN-05-10022.pdf Other good IRS resources:

We can , and we do . AMD 's 24 - core EPYC CPUs have 19.2 billion transistors , while Nvidia 's Volta workstation GPU has 21.1 billion . You could easily just string a bunch together in a server rack to make a brain sized computer . The problem is the brain does n't work the same way a computer does . Transistors have two states : high and low . Showing as a binary 1 or 0 . Neurons are analogue devices that can make multiple connections and use a variety of different neurotransmitters to bounce data around . The brain is also " plastic " . It can change its structure to suit its needs . The more you run a routine through it , the brain physically adapts to strengthen neural connections , so you run that routine more efficiently . Remember learning to ride a bike as a kid ? Or learning to drive a car ? Pretty difficult , eh . But now you can probably do it without thinking . What we call " muscle memory " or " running on autopilot " , that 's neural plasticity at work . Computers on the other hand do n't have this advantage . When you design a processor , that processor 's structure is going to remain the same for the entirety of its lifespan . You can build machines that can " learn " through software , but the static nature of hardware is still a limitation . There are software limitations as well since computers execute tasks differently than the brain does . Computers need to be taught every single step in a process with precision in order to function , where as the brain can sort of consolidate tasks based on what it already knows . Now there are ways to optimize computer code through shortcuts to increase efficiency , but this will only take you so far . So while computers can perform some tasks a lot faster than the brain , the brain is in many ways more efficient . Computers are good at quickly processing large but simple data sets , while the brain is far better for processing complex , abstract tasks .

Surprisingly , one of the things the Norman Conquest did n't affect very much is the language , so I 'm disputing /u / crummocks 's post here . In fact , the effect on the language was mostly limited to adding some words for things the Normans put themselves in charge of : government , royalty , the courts , the military and so on , plus a few other random bits and pieces , such as the words for some meats ( beef and pork , for example , but not chicken or lamb ) . It is a convenient date to put the transition from Old English to Middle English , but please dismiss from your mind any suggestion that this means there was any kind of dramatic change in the language . Like all languages , English is continually and gradually evolving and changing : there was n't a sudden massive change , and transitions happened very slowly . The English spoken in 1065 was no different from the English spoken in 1067 , but is quite different from the English spoken in 800 . What really sets Middle English apart from Old English is that it 's considerably more analytic . Old English was synthetic , meaning you could put the words in almost any order , but there was a complicated system of grammatical endings ; an analytic language is one where you have far fewer grammatical endings , but the word order is very strict . The influence of Norman French probably helped with that process , but it likely would have happened anyhow -- you can see this process happening in modern German , for example , while in modern Dutch it 's practically complete . In any case , that change did n't really get going for another couple of generations at least , which is why some language historians put the transition to Middle English closer to 1150 .

The answer to this question is a bit complicated but can be understood by anyone . First you need a few assumptions : * The speed of light is the same for all reference frames . Another way of saying that is no matter what you are looking at you always measure the speed of light the same . * You are always not moving in your own reference frame . Another way of saying it is , if you are in a car looking out the window it means exactly the same thing to say " you and the car are moving down the road " and " the road is moving below you . " One more thing . You need to know how clocks work . Since the one thing that is always the same is the speed of light , the simplest clock is a light clock . It is a flashlight / sensor ( FS ) and a mirror ( _ ) FS _ _ The flashlight makes a flash of light . The light bounces off the mirror and then hits the sensor . That is one tick . Each second is just a certain number of ticks . FS || || _ || _ If you hold a clock and look at it you would see the light taking a path straight up and down . Now let 's think what someone else would see if they looked at your clock while you drive down the high way . The flashlight / sensor would n't be in the same place when they made the flash as when it bounced back so to them it would look more like this . car moving this way - > F S \ / \ / _ _ \/ _ _ Notice that instead of going straight up and down the light had to go diagonal . \ is a longer path than | . Since the speed of light is always the same and the light when a longer distance , it must have taken more time . For the person looking at your light clock each tick takes a longer time . Their own clock records more ticks in a second then yours does . Now this does n't happen to you . You are always at rest in your frame so the light is still bouncing straight up and down for you . You have no reason to think that your clock is running slowly . In fact . If you look out the windows at the other person 's clock you see them standing on the side of the road moving backwards . Their clock runs slowly for exactly the same reason . Time goes slower for people outside of your own frame because light has to travel a longer distance .

In this type of strategy profit is made when the shares go down as your main position is the short trade of the common stock. The convertible instruments will tend to move in about the same direction as the underlying (what it can be converted to) but less violently as they are traded less (lower volatility and lower volume in the market on both sides), however, they are not being used to make a profit so much as to hedge against the stock going up. Since both the bonds and the preference shares are higher on the list to be repaid if the company declares bankruptcy and the bonds pay out a fixed amount of interest as well, both also help protect against problems that may occur with a long position in the common stock.  Essentially the plan with this strategy is to earn fixed income on the bonds whilst the stock price drops and then to sell both the bonds and buy the stock back on the market to cover the short position. If the prediction that the stock will fall is wrong then you are still earning fixed income on the debt and are able to convert it into stock at the higher price to cover the short sale eliminating, or reducing, the loss made on the short sale. Effectively the profit here is made on the spread between the price of the bond, accounting for the conversion price, and the price of the stock and that fixed income is less volatile (except usually in the junk market) than stock.

First , voter fraud is already extremely rare . Here 's why . Only people who are registered to vote are allowed to vote . When you register , you have to provide a social security number , driver 's license number , or some other proof of citizenship . There are several checks and balances made to make it difficult to fraudulently register . When you vote , you have to provide the name of someone who 's registered to vote . They check that every person only votes once . In rare cases where the same person tries to vote twice , or someone tries to vote in place of someone else , this is easily caught and they investigate and throw out the invalid ballots . Between 2000 and 2010 [ there were only 13 cases of in - person voter fraud ] ( URL_0 ) , where someone either tried to vote twice or claimed they were someone else . Only 13 ! And all of them were caught , no fraudulent votes were ever counted . So the first part of the answer is , voter fraud is so rare that we do n't need new measures to prevent it ! Here 's the other part of the answer : Approximately 10 % of U.S. Citizens do not have a photo ID . Many do n't need it or do n't want it . Photo IDs usually cost money , but voting is free . So requiring photo ID does n't prevent any fraud , but it does either exclude some people from voting , or adds a financial burden for them to get the photo ID they otherwise do n't need . Why do Democrats oppose it more ? Because most of the people who do n't have an ID vote Democratic . If the people who do n't have an ID voted Republican , you 'd probably see some Democrats try to add voter ID laws and Republicans oppose it - but that would n't make it right . An unbiased analysis still shows that requiring photo ID is not * necessary * .

There are a couple ways to think about diarrhea . The way the gut moves shit through it is called gastric motility and is controlled by a couple of things : * what 's inside the gut ( water , sugar , fat , protein ) * hormones acting on the gut ( which change in response to food and stress ) * electrical signals ( kind of like the heart , the gut has rhythmic contractions . These can be dysregulated and result in problems with gut motility ) There are a lot of causes of diarrhea . * Suddenly loading the stomach / small intestine with a high VOLUME load can cause rapid emptying . For example , this occurs in dumping syndrome , and yes , it can be caused by drinking a shitload of water ! ! * A large load of sugary or salty food / beverage into the GI can cause an OSMOTIC load . This means that water rushes into the intestine and WHOOSH . Etc . This is called osmotic diarrhea . * Gut motility can be really messed up after bowel surgery . A normal gut can tell the brain " there 's food here " and the brain can stimulate the release of hormones that move things along . However , removing parts of the GI , or not using them for a long time can mess up these pathways and cause both constipation and diarrhea . * Stimulant drugs can cause diarrhea . * There are viral causes of diarrhea which affect ( usually ) kids or older folks in ( often ) developing countries . * Inflammatory GI disorders like Crohn 's or UC can cause diarrhea . Often it 's osmotic diarrhea which happens due to gut inflammation . However , the gut also shows hypercontractility ( lots of movement ) in these disorders for reasons which are not well understood , and this can cause diarrhea . * The gut is like the heart in that it has its own pacemaker cells and rhythmic contractions . You 've heard of cardiac arrhythmias ? That 's where the heart does n't beat properly because of electrical issues in the tissues . The gut can also develop these problems , for example in diabetes when the nerves get worn out all over the body . This can contribute to diarrhea ( or constipation ) depending on where / when it occurs .

Video games,[a] also known as computer games, are electronic games that involves interaction with a user interface or input device – such as a joystick, controller, keyboard, or motion sensing device – to generate visual feedback. This feedback mostly commonly is shown on a video display device, such as a TV set, monitor, touchscreen, or virtual reality headset. Some computer games do not always depend on a graphics display, for example text adventure games and computer chess can be played through teletype printers. Video games are often augmented with audio feedback delivered through speakers or headphones, and sometimes with other types of feedback, including haptic technology.
Video games are defined based on their platform, which include arcade video games, console games, and personal computer (PC) games. More recently, the industry has expanded onto mobile gaming through smartphones and tablet computers, virtual and augmented reality systems, and remote cloud gaming. Video games are classified into a wide range of genres based on their type of gameplay and purpose.
The first video game prototypes in the 1950s and 1960s were simple extensions of electronic games using video-like output from large room-size computers. The first consumer video game was the arcade video game Computer Space in 1971. In 1972 came the iconic hit arcade game Pong, and the first home console, the Magnavox Odyssey. The industry grew quickly during the golden age of arcade video games from the late 1970s to early 1980s, but suffered from the crash of the North American video game market in 1983 due to loss of publishing control and saturation of the market. Following the crash, the industry matured, dominated by Japanese companies such as Nintendo, Sega, and Sony, and established practices and methods around the development and distribution of video games to prevent a similar crash in the future, many of which continue to be followed. Today, video game development requires numerous skills to bring a game to market, including developers, publishers, distributors, retailers, console and other third-party manufacturers, and other roles.
In the 2000s, the core industry centered on "AAA" games, leaving little room for riskier, experimental games. Coupled with the availability of the Internet and digital distribution, this gave room for independent video game development (or indie games) to gain prominence into the 2010s. Since then, the commercial importance of the video game industry has been increasing. The emerging Asian markets and mobile games on smartphones in particular are altering player demographics towards casual gaming and increasing monetization by incorporating games as a service. As of 2020, the global video game market has estimated annual revenues of US$159 billion across hardware, software, and services. This is three times the size of the 2019 global music industry and four times that of the 2019 film industry.

There is actually a lot of competition for hospital services , and some hospitals make a point of specializing in one area . Rarely will you see an advertisement for a hospital that says " We 're a great place to get better . " No , they are specific : We have a great cardio center , we focus on ( often specific types of ) cancer , specific surgeries , etc . Now , if you have an emergency this is n't an issue : you 're going to go to the closest place to get that sorted out . But if you 've been diagnosed with lung cancer you 're going to want to shop around . Unfortunately , this sort of competition has three very consequences : meaningless rankings , an arms race for technology , and increased prices . Hospital rankings need to be taken with a grain of salt . Some just came out , and they are usually used in advertising . I mean , if U.S. News & World Report ranked you high in something , you 'd brag about it , right ? But the methodology ( is using the word " methodology " okay in ELI5 ? ) is [ really flawed ] ( URL_0 ) and the results are meaningless . It also means that hospitals are investing in expensive equipment that they might not need . Since these rankings , and the ads that follow , are based on methodology that examines equipment , facilities , and staff it leads to places making expensive acquisitions that they may really not need . So both these two factors ( along with many others ) lead to increased prices for consumers . This equipment needs to be paid for , these hotshot doctors need a bonus , and hey , the administrator did such a great job getting us onto that ( meaningless ) list he should get a bonus , too . I am not saying that these are the only things jacking up prices , that list is a mile long , but they are contributing factors . edit : a few words

Helium is not flammable , actually . Gases are flammable ( and reactive ) in a general sense when they do not have full valence shells . Electrons like to orbit atoms in groups , depending on how far away from the center they are . Helium is the smallest of the elements that are pretty much good on their own ; the smallest valence shell is 2 electrons , and that 's how many helium has . The next up is Neon with 10 , and he 's pretty cool by himself to . full valence shells means they ca n't really make compounds with anything . You hear a lot about carbon dioxide and H2O , but not about Helium Dioxide . When you burn hydrogen , what 's happening is you add a small amount of energy to the system , and the hydrogens find some oxygen buddies in the air and link up . This creates water , and releases energy . However , this ca n't take place by itself ; the hydrogen is too shy . You have to add a match to the whole system to excite enough hydrogen to link up , and the others will follow suit . In chemistry , this is called a combustion reaction . Combustion reactions generally take place between oxygen and those things that will allow their hydrogens to chill with oxygen , becoming water . Most of the alcohols ( methanol , ethanol , propane , butane , etc . ) Have carbon atoms and a bunch of hydrogen atoms they can usually part with . Explosive reactions may take place without hydrogen and oxygen present , but I do n't believe they are considered combustion reactions . Hope that helps

First of all , when you ' steal ' data , you do n't actually ' steal ' it . You replicate it . Unless you work at a highly secured site , the IT professionals are n't likely to track access down to this level . If the Vice President of Marketing wants to download 1.5 TB worth of Game of Thrones scripts , so be it . At most , your standard IT department will track * traffic * to see if there 's unusual activity - but most of the activity they track will be external ( i.e. : over the Internet ) and if you 've penetrated the company deeply enough , they can distribute the load across many accounts . In terms of ' how they do it ' , normally it 's an issue of sneaking malicious code onto machines on the internal network . Most people who use computers professionally do not take particularly good security precautions , on the presumption that their IT department deals with that . IT departments at low - security places like HBO also tend to prioritize convenience over security . So what happens is that a company like Google , Apple or Microsoft is informed / discovers an exploit . They keep this private until they issue a patch to correct it . At which point the exploit is public knowledge but many people will not yet have the patch to protect against it . Since updating an entire company 's worth computers is a massive nuisance not only to the IT department but also the users of those computers , you 'll often see companies going weeks or months without critical security patches . You also generally ca n't lock the barn door after the cows are gone . Once the malware has gotten in through the exploit , it 's there . Fixing the exploit wo n't remove the malware . Detecting the malware is also a very reactive process . Only once someone has found and submitted malware to a database can most anti - virus programs detect it - at which point it 's almost certainly too late for the people who were initially targeted . There are also more subtle tricks you can use . For example , a company like HBO almost certainly has regular backups of their servers . There 's a good chance that some element of this backup process electronically submits the information to a remote server . If you 've got malware in the system , you can simply redirect the backup to your own computer and then forward it to their server . This is similar to a man - in - the - middle attack ( although not strictly so , since it involves compromising one end of the communications ) . With such an approach , the internal IT department ca n't even tell from traffic charts what is going on - they expected that large transfer of data . The fundamental issue is that good security is actually quite easy - it 's just incredibly inconvenient . Think about it in terms of physical security . If you want to make sure no one breaks into your home , you can enclose your home in a solid concrete bunker with no openings . Anyone who wants to boost your TV will have to do so with jackhammers and wake the entire neighborhood . But actually * living * in a house entirely contained within a no - access concrete shell would be a nuisance . The same is true with electronic security . Highly secured sites are also incredibly inconvenient sites . If you work at a secured site , you 'll have to surrender your phone and all electronics ( including items like flash drives ) when you enter . You ca n't browse the web while you 're in the building because there are no connections to the larger Internet . If you want to take home some work , you 'll have to fill out paperwork in triplicate just so some IT guy will get around to giving you a copy a few weeks from now . HBO executives do n't want to live like that , so they end up with their data stolen every now and then .
